\subsection*{6.3.4.4.2 MIMO midamble}

MIMO midamble is used for PMI selection and CQI calculation in closed-loop MIMO. For OL MIMO, midamble can be used to calculate CQI. MIMO midamble shall be transmitted every frame on the second DL AAI subframe. The midamble signal occupies the first OFDMA symbol in a DL Type 1 or Type 2 AAI subframe. For the Type 1 AAI subframe case, the remaining five consecutive symbols form a Type 3 AAI subframe. For the Type 2 AAI subframe case, the remaining six consecutive symbols form a Type 1 AAI subframe.

For the special case where the frame structure supports R1 MSs and Frame Configuration Index provisions that the DL Length is equal to 1 subframe, the MIMO midamble will be transmitted every frame on the last symbol of the first DL AAI subframe. In these cases, the first DL AAI subframe will always be a Type 2 AAI subframe. The first symbol of this Type 2 AAI subframe will be occupied by a preamble symbol, and the last symbol will be occupied by the MIMO midamble. The remaining five consecutive symbols form a Type 3 AAI subframe.

The MIMO midamble signal transmitted by the ABS antenna is defined by Equation (211):
$$
\begin{equation*}
s(t)=\operatorname{Re}\left\{e^{j 2 \pi f_{c} t} \sum_{\substack{k=N_{\text {used }}-1 \\ k=0}} b_{k} \cdot e^{j 2 \pi\left(k-\frac{N_{\text {used }}-1}{2}\right) \Delta f\left(t-T_{g}\right)}\right\} \tag{211}
\end{equation*}
$$
where $b_{k}$ is a coefficients modulating subcarriers in the midamble symbol defined by Equation (212):
$$
b_{k}=\left\{\begin{array}{l}
2.18 \cdot\{1-(2 G([k+u+\text { offset }(f f t)] \bmod f f t))\}, k \neq \frac{N_{u s e d}-1}{2},(k-s) \bmod \left(3 \times N_{t}\right)=3 g+\left(\left\lfloor\frac{I D C e l l}{256}\right\rfloor+\left\lfloor\frac{k-s}{N_{1} \times P_{S C}}\right\rfloor\right) \bmod 3  \tag{212}\\
0, \text { otherwise }
\end{array}\right.
$$
where $k$ is the subcarrier index ( $0 \leq k \leq N_{\text {used }}-1$ ); $N_{\text {used }}$ is the number of used subcarriers; $G(x)$ is the Golay sequence defined in Table 6-164 ( $0 \leq x \leq 2047$ ) ; fft is the FFT size used; $u$ is a shift value, where the actual value of $u$ is derived from $u=\bmod ($ IDcell, 256); offset(fft) is an FFT size specific offset as defined in Table 6-165; $g$ is an ABS transmit antenna index (in the range of 0 to $N_{t}-1$ ); $N_{t}$ is the number of ABS transmit antennas; and parameter $s=0$, for $k \leq\left(N_{\text {used }}-1\right) / 2$, and $s=1$, for $k>\left(N_{\text {used }}-1\right) / 2$. IDcell shall correspond to SA-Preamble.

Example of physical structure of MIMO midamble is shown in Figure 6-133 for 4Tx antenna and IDcell $=0$.

\includegraphics[alt={},max width=\textwidth]{https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/b75642f5-8f88-4574-9a75-f32eadecb271-0613.jpg?height=600&width=1509&top_left_y=1598&top_left_x=309}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Figure 6-133-MIMO midamble physical structure for 4TX antennas and IDcell $=0$}
\end{figure}

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline 0xEDE2 & 0xED1D & 0xEDE2 & 0x12E2 & 0xEDE2 & 0xED1D & 0x121D & 0xED1D & 0xEDE2 & 0xED1D & 0xEDE2 & 0x12E2 \\
\hline 0x121D & 0x12E2 & 0xEDE2 & 0x12E2 & 0xEDE2 & 0xED1D & 0xEDE2 & 0x12E2 & 0xEDE2 & 0xED1D & 0x121D & 0xED1D \\
\hline 0x121D & 0x12E2 & 0x121D & 0xED1D & 0xEDE2 & 0xED1D & 0x121D & 0xED1D & 0xEDE2 & 0xED1D & 0xEDE2 & 0x12E2 \\
\hline 0xEDE2 & 0xED1D & 0x121D & 0xED1D & 0xEDE2 & 0xED1D & 0xEDE2 & 0x12E2 & 0x121D & 0x12E2 & 0xEDE2 & 0x12E2 \\
\hline 0x121D & 0x12E2 & 0x121D & 0xED1D & 0x121D & 0x12E2 & 0xEDE2 & 0x12E2 & 0xEDE2 & 0xED1D & 0xEDE2 & 0x12E2 \\
\hline 0x121D & 0x12E2 & 0xEDE2 & 0x12E2 & 0xEDE2 & 0xED1D & 0xEDE2 & 0x12E2 & 0xEDE2 & 0xED1D & 0x121D & 0xED1D \\
\hline 0xEDE2 & 0xED1D & 0xEDE2 & 0x12E2 & 0x121D & 0x12E2 & 0xEDE2 & 0x12E2 & 0xEDE2 & 0xED1D & 0xEDE2 & 0x12E2 \\
\hline 0xEDE2 & 0xED1D & 0x121D & 0xED1D & 0x121D & 0x12E2 & 0x121D & 0xED1D & 0xEDE2 & 0xED1D & 0x121D & 0xED1D \\
\hline 0x121D & 0x12E2 & 0x121D & 0xED1D & 0x121D & 0x12E2 & 0xEDE2 & 0x12E2 & 0x121D & 0x12E2 & 0x121D & 0xED1D \\
\hline 0xEDE2 & 0xED1D & 0x121D & 0xED1D & 0xEDE2 & 0xED1D & 0xEDE2 & 0x12E2 & 0xEDE2 & 0xED1D & 0x121D & 0xED1D \\
\hline 0x121D & 0x12E2 & 0x121D & 0xED1D & 0xEDE2 & 0xED1D & 0x121D & 0xED1D & & & & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-165-Offsets in the Golay sequence}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|}
\hline FFT size & Offset \\
\hline 2048 & 30 \\
\hline 1024 & 60 \\
\hline 512 & 40 \\
\hline

In Table 6-164, the hexadecimal series should be read as a sequence of bits where each 16-bit word is started at the MSB and ends at the LSB where the second word MSB follows. The first bit of the sequence is referenced as offset 0 .

\subsection*{6.3.4.4.3 Usage of downlink pilots}

The demodulation pilots in a given PRU on a given pilot stream shall be precoded the same way as the data transmitted on the same stream in that PRU. In DLRU, the data transmitted in a given PRU on a given stream may be sent to several AMSs but in different tones using the same precoder.

Two pilot streams shall always be transmitted in the DLRUs, whether inside or outside the open-loop region Type 0, and whether or not data is being transmitted by the ABS in all DLRUs.

Inside an open-loop region of Type 1 or Type 2, the $\operatorname{Max} M_{t}$ pilots shall always be transmitted across all CLRUs in that open-loop region. Outside an open-loop region, the pilots shall not be transmitted on CLRUs where no data is sent.

The precoder may be adaptive (user-specific) or non-adaptive (non-user-specific) depending on the DL MIMO mode. Non-adaptive precoders are determined according to the DL MIMO mode, the number of streams, the type of LRU, the operation inside or outside the open-loop region, and the physical index of the subband or miniband where the precoder is applied.

In MU-MIMO transmissions in CLRU, each pilot stream is dedicated to one AMS. A pilot stream dedicated to an AMS shall be used by the AMS for channel estimation within the allocation. Pilot streams dedicated to other AMSs may be used for inter-stream interference estimation. The total number of streams in the transmission and the index of the dedicated pilot stream are indicated in the DL Basic Assignment A-MAP IE, DL Persistent Allocation A-MAP IE, or DL Subband Assignment A-MAP IE.

Channel estimation for demodulation of data burst at the AMS should be performed as follows:
- In DLRU: The 2-streams non-adaptively precoded common pilots across the DLRU should be used for channel estimation by all AMSs allocated a burst in the DLRU. Within each frequency partition, all pilots are shared by all AMSs for demodulation in DLRU.
- In CLRU: The AMS should use its dedicated pilot streams for channel estimation in the allocation. Pilots are not shared by AMSs for demodulation in CLRU, whether they are non-adaptively or adaptively precoded.

MIMO feedback measurements at the AMS should be performed as follows:
- For MIMO feedback reports requested with a MIMO feedback mode for operation in an open-loop region, measurements should be taken on the $\operatorname{Max} M_{t}$ streams non-adaptively precoded pilots in that open-loop region. All pilots are shared by all AMSs for MIMO feedback measurements in each open-loop region.
- Wideband CQI reports inside OL region should be averaged over OL region pilots of the PRUs in the frequency partition 0.
- For MIMO feedback reports requested with a MIMO feedback mode for operation outside the openloop region, measurements should be taken on the downlink MIMO midamble.
- Wideband CQI reports outside OL region (measured on MIMO midamble) should be averaged over the frequency partition as instructed in 6.3.5.5.2.4.5or as defined in 6.3.5.5.2.4.11.
- For reports requested with a MIMO feedback mode for open-loop MIMO operation, the AMS should adjust the non-precoded MIMO channel estimated from the midamble by applying it with the nonadaptive precoder according to the MFM, the subband index, and assumption on STC rate.
- For reports requested with a MIMO feedback mode for closed-loop MIMO operation, the AMS should adjust the non-precoded MIMO channel estimated from the midamble with an estimated adaptive precoder.
- Subband CQI reports (inside and outside OL region) should be reported for subbands in SLRUs indicated by SFH.

\subsection*{6.3.4.4.4 E-MBS zone-specific pilot patterns}

E-MBS zone-specific pilots are transmitted for multi-cell multicast broadcast single frequency network (MBSFN) transmissions. An E-MBS zone is a group of ABSs involved in an SFN transmission. The E-MBS zone-specific pilots are common inside one E-MBS zone. Synchronous transmission of the same pilots from multiple ABSs helps an AMS to estimate the composite channel response within an E-MBS zone.

The E-MBS pilot pattern is robust for various cell size MBSFN deployments and supports channel estimation of up to two transmit streams within an E-MBS zone. The structures of the E-MBS zone specific pilot pattern for Type 1 subframes are shown in Figure 6-134.

The E-MBS zone specific pilot pattern for Type 2 subframes is derived from the E-MBS zone specific pilot pattern for Type 1 subframes by duplicating the second symbol and appending to the end of the last symbol.

The E-MBS zone specific pilot pattern for Type 3 subframes is derived from the E-MBS zone specific pilot pattern for Type 1 subframes by removing the fourth symbol.

Figure 6-135 and Figure 6-136 show the E-MBS zone specific pilot patterns for Type 2 and Type 3 subframes, respectively.

\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Figure 6-134-E-MBS zone specific pilot patterns for 1 and 2 stream transmission in Type 1 subframe}
\end{figure}

\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}

\subsection*{6.3.4.5 Downlink physical structure for multicarrier support}

Guard subcarriers between carriers form integer multiples of PRUs. The structure of guard PRUs are the same as the structure defined in 6.3.4.1 and 6.3.4.4. The guard PRUs are used as miniband CRUs at partition FP0 for data transmission only. The number of usable guard subcarriers is predefined and should be known to both the AMS and the ABS based on carrier bandwidth. The number of guard PRUs on the left and right edge of each carrier are shown in Table 6-166. Denote the number of guard PRUs on the left (right) edge of the carrier by $N_{\text {LGPRU }}\left(N_{\text {RGPRU }}\right)$. The total number of guard PRUs are $N_{\text {GPRU }}=N_{\text {LGPRU }}+N_{\text {RGPRU }}$.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-166-Number of guard PRUs}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline $\boldsymbol{B} \boldsymbol{W}$ & Number of guard PRUs on the left edge of carrier ${ }^{\text {a }}$ & Number of guard PRUs on the right edge of carrier ${ }^{\text {b }}$ \\
\hline 5 MHz & 0 & 0 \\
\hline 10 MHz & 1 & 1 \\
\hline 20 MHz & 2 & 2 \\
\hline 7 MHz & 0 & 0 \\
\hline 8.75 MHz & 0 & 0 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\footnotetext{
${ }^{\mathrm{a}}$ When a carrier occupies the left most spectrum among multiple contiguous carriers, the number of guard PRUs on the left edge of the carrier is zero.
${ }^{\mathrm{b}}$ When a carrier occupies the right most spectrum among multiple contiguous carriers, the number of guard PRUs on the right edge of the carrier is zero.
}

Denote left guard PRUs and right guard PRUs by $\operatorname{GPRU}_{\mathrm{L}}[0], \ldots . \operatorname{GPRU}_{\mathrm{L}}\left[N_{L G P R U}-1\right]$ and $\operatorname{GPRU}_{\mathrm{R}}[0], \ldots$. $\operatorname{GPRU}_{\mathrm{R}}\left[N_{R G P R U}-1\right]$ from the lowest frequency. Then, guard PRUs are indexed by interleaving GPRU ${ }_{\mathrm{L}}$ and $\operatorname{GPRU}_{\mathrm{R}}$ one by one. That is, $\operatorname{GPRU}[i]=\operatorname{GPRU}_{\mathrm{L}}[i / 2]$, for $i$ is an even number and $\operatorname{GPRU}[i]=\operatorname{GPRU}_{\mathrm{R}}[(i-$ 1)/2], for $i$ is an odd number, If $N_{L G P R U}=0$, then $\operatorname{GPRU}[i]=\operatorname{GPRU}_{\mathrm{R}}[i]$. If $N_{R G P R U}=0$, then $\operatorname{GPRU}[i]= \operatorname{GPRU}_{L}[i]$.

The $\mathrm{N}_{\text {GPRU }}$ guard PRUs are used as miniband LRUs, i.e., NLRUs at frequency partition FP0 without any permutation for data transmission only. In detail, $i$-th guard NLRU, i.e., GNLRU[ $i$ ] is always allocated along with the $i$-th last NLRU at partition $\mathrm{FP}_{0}$. In other words, when an allocation including the $i$-th last NLRU at partition $\mathrm{FP}_{0}$ is made to an AMS for multicarrier support, the $i$-th guard NLRU, i.e., GNLRU[i], is allocated together. The mapping to the GNLRU[i] is made after mapping to the $i$-th NLRU at partition $\mathrm{FP}_{0}$.

When an adjacent carrier is not an active carrier of the AMS, the guard subcarriers in between active and non-active carriers shall not be utilized for data transmission for that AMS.

When the overlapped guard subcarriers are not aligned in the frequency domain, they shall not be used for data transmission.

\subsection*{6.3.4.6 Downlink physical structure for E-MBS support}

In a carrier that is not dedicated to E-MBS traffic, it may be required to multiplex E-MBS and unicast traffic. When multiplexed with unicast data, the E-MBS traffic is FDMed with the unicast traffic in the downlink subframes. More specifically, the E-MBS traffic is transmitted using subbands assigned in frequency partition $F P_{0}$. The first AAI subframe in every superframe transmits SFH and does not contain any subbands. Therefore, no E-MBS traffic is scheduled in the first AAI subframe of every superframe. The number of subband logical resource units (SLRUs) used for E-MBS data, denoted by $K_{\text {SB, E-MBS }}$, is indicated in the Zone Allocation BitMAP in the AAI-SCD message. This set of subbands form the E-MBS region in the downlink subframes. The Zone Allocation BitMAP is valid for the set of superframes over which the AAI-E-MBS_CFG indicators are valid. If frequency partition $F P_{0}$ is available, $K_{S B, E-M B S}$ subbands are assigned from the lowest $\mathrm{SLRU}_{\mathrm{E}-\mathrm{MBS}}$ in frequency partition $F P_{0}$. If not, however, $K_{S B, E-M B S}$ subbands are assigned equitably to the remaining partitions FPi (i>0).

The mapping between SLRUE-MBS index and the PRU index shall be done as follows:
If $F P_{0}$ is used, only the subbands from $F P_{0}$ shall be assigned for E-MBS and the SLRU $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{E}-\mathrm{MBS}}$ index is determined by
$$
\operatorname{SLRU}_{\mathrm{E}-\mathrm{MBS}}[k]=\operatorname{SLRU}_{\mathrm{FP} 0}[k], \text { for } 0 \quad k \leq N_{1} \times K_{S B, E-M B S}
$$

In case the $F P_{0}$ is not available (e.g., reuse-3 only), the number of PRUs for E-MBS in each frequency partition, FPi ( $i>0$ ), is derived as Equation (213).
$$
L_{E M B S, F P m}= \begin{cases}N_{1} \cdot\left\lceil\frac{K_{S B, E M B S}}{F P C T}\right\rceil & m<q+1  \tag{213}\\ N_{1} \cdot\left\lceil\frac{K_{S B, E M B S}}{F P C T}\right\rfloor & \text { Otherwise }\end{cases}
$$
where
$$
q=K_{S B, E-M B S}-\mathrm{FPCT} \times \text { floor }\left(K_{S B, E-M B S} / \mathrm{FPCT}\right), 1 \leq m<\mathrm{FPCT}
$$

For each frequency partition $i(i>0)$, the total number of PRUs for E-MBS up to and including that partition may be calculated as Equation (214).
$$
\begin{equation*}
X_{i}=\sum_{m=1}^{i} L_{E M B S, F P m} \quad 1 \leq i<F P C T \quad \text { and } \quad X_{0}=0 \tag{214}
\end{equation*}
$$

Then, the $\mathrm{SLRU}_{\mathrm{E}-\mathrm{MBS}}$ index is given by
$$
\operatorname{SLRU}_{\mathrm{E}-\mathrm{MBS}}\left[k+X_{i-1}\right]=\operatorname{SLRU}_{\mathrm{FPi}}[k], \text { for } 0 \leq k<\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{EMBS}, \mathrm{FPi}} \text { with } 1 \leq i<\mathrm{FPCT}
$$
where the mapping from the $\operatorname{SLRU}_{\mathrm{FPi}}$ to the PRU indices is as specified in 6.3.4.3.5. The subbands index for E-MBS is determined by
$S B_{E M B S}[m]=\left\{\right.$ AllSLR $U_{E M B S}[k]$, with indices $k$ such that $\left.\left\lfloor\frac{k}{N_{1}}\right\rfloor=m\right\}$ with $0 \leq m<\mathrm{K}_{S B, E M B S}$.

\subsection*{6.3.5 Downlink control structure}

\subsection*{6.3.5.1 Advanced Preamble}

There are two types of Advanced Preamble (A-Preamble): Primary Advanced Preamble (PA-Preamble) and Secondary Advanced Preamble (SA-Preamble). One PA-Preamble symbol and two SA-Preamble symbols exist within the superframe. The location of the A-Preamble symbol is specified as the first symbol of the frame except for the last frame. PA-Preamble is located at the first symbol of the second frame in a superframe, while SA-Preamble is located at the first symbol of the first and the third frames. Figure 6-137 depicts the location of A-Preamble symbols.

\begin{figure}
\includegraphics[alt={},max width=\textwidth]{https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/b75642f5-8f88-4574-9a75-f32eadecb271-0619.jpg?height=577&width=1004&top_left_y=1482&top_left_x=567}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Figure 6-137-Location of the A-Preamble}
\end{figure}

\subsection*{6.3.5.1.1 Primary Advanced Preamble (PA-Preamble)}

The length of sequence for the PA-Preamble is 216 regardless of the FFT size. PA-Preamble carries the information of system bandwidth and carrier configuration. When the subcarrier index 256 is reserved for DC, the allocation of subcarriers is accomplished by Equation (215).
$$
\begin{equation*}
\text { PAPreambleCarrierSet }=2 k+41 \tag{215}
\end{equation*}
$$
where
$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\text { PAPreambleCarrierSet } & \text { specifies all subcarriers allocated to the PA-Preamble. } \\
k & \text { is a running index } 0 \text { to } 215 .
\end{array}
$$

Figure 6-138 depicts the symbol structure of the PA-Preamble in the frequency domain.

\begin{figure}
\includegraphics[alt={},max width=\textwidth]{https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/b75642f5-8f88-4574-9a75-f32eadecb271-0620.jpg?height=381&width=1299&top_left_y=975&top_left_x=414}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Figure 6-138-PA-Preamble symbol structure}
\end{figure}

In Table 6-167, the sequence of the PA-Preamble is defined in a hexadecimal format. The defined series is mapped onto subcarriers in ascending order. The value of the series is obtained by converting the series to a binary series and starting the series from the MSB up to 216 bits ( 0 mapped to +1 and 1 mapped to -1 ).

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-167-PA-Preamble series}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Index & Carrier & BW & Series to modulate \\
\hline 0 & Fully configured & 5 MHz & 6DB4F3B16BCE59166C9CEF7C3C8CA5EDFC16 A9D1DC01F2AE6AA08F \\
\hline 1 & Fully configured & 7, 8.75, and 10 MHz & 1799628F3B9F8F3B22C1BA19EAF94FEC4D37D EE97E027750D298AC \\
\hline 2 & Fully configured & 20 MHz & 92161C7C19BB2FC0ADE5CEF3543AC1B6CE6B E1C8DCABDDD319EAF7 \\
\hline 3 & Fully configured & Reserved & 6DE116E665C395ADC70A89716908620868A603 40BF35ED547F8281 \\
\hline 4 & Fully configured & Reserved & BCFDF60DFAD6B027E4C39DB20D783C9F4671 55179CBA31115E2D04 \\
\hline 5 & Fully configured & Reserved & 7EF1379553F9641EE6ECDBF5F144287E329606 C616292A3C77F928 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-167-PA-Preamble series (continued)}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Index & Carrier & BW & Series to modulate \\
\hline 6 & Fully configured & Reserved & 8A9CA262B8B3D37E3158A3B17BFA4C9FCFF4 D396D2A93DE65A0E7C \\
\hline 7 & Fully configured & Reserved & DA8CE648727E4282780384AB53CEEBD1CBF79 E0C5DA7BA85DD3749 \\
\hline 8 & Fully configured & Reserved & 3A65D1E6042E8B8AADC701E210B5B4B650B6 AB31F7A918893FB04A \\
\hline 9 & Fully configured & Reserved & D46CF86FE51B56B2CAA84F26F6F204428C1BD 23F3D888737A0851C \\
\hline 10 & Partially configured & N/A & 640267A0C0DF11E475066F1610954B5AE55E189 EA7E72EFD57240F \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The sequences of indexes from 3 to 9 in Table 6-167 are reserved for the irregular nominal channel bandwidth to support tone dropping.

The magnitude boosting levels for different FFT sizes are shown in Table 6-168.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-168-PA-Preamble boosting levels}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline $\mathbf{5 1 2}$ & $\mathbf{1 k}$ & $\mathbf{2 k}$ \\
\hline 1.9216 & 2.6731 & 4.6511 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

For 512-FFT, the boosted PA-Preamble at the $k$-th subcarrier can be written as
$$
c_{k}=\frac{1.9216}{\sqrt{N}_{t}} \times b_{k}
$$
where $b_{k}$ represents the PA-Preamble before boosting ( +1 or -1 ) and $N_{t}$ is the number of transmit antennas.
In the case where the Advanced Air Interface supports R1 MSs in mixed mode as shown in Figure 6-139, the A-Preamble symbol with a different time domain waveform from the WirelessMAN-OFDMA R1 Reference System preamble should be transmitted with an offset of an integer number of symbols in the first WirelessMAN-OFDMA R1 Reference System DL zone, as specified in 6.3.3.5.1.

\begin{figure}
\includegraphics[alt={},max width=\textwidth]{https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/b75642f5-8f88-4574-9a75-f32eadecb271-0622.jpg?height=604&width=1430&top_left_y=348&top_left_x=352}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Figure 6-139-A-Preamble transmission structure supporting WirelessMAN-OFDMA}
\end{figure}

\subsection*{6.3.5.1.2 Secondary advanced preamble (SA-Preamble)}

The $N_{S A P}$, the number of subcarriers allocated for SA-Preamble, are 144, 288, and 576 for 512-FFT, 1024FFT, and 2048-FFT, respectively. The allocation of subcarriers is accomplished by Equation (216), when the subcarrier indexes 256, 512, and 1024 are reserved for DC for 512-FFT, 1024-FFT, and 2048-FFT, respectively.
$$
\begin{equation*}
\text { SAPreambleCarrierSet }_{n}=n+3 \cdot k+40 \cdot \frac{N_{S A P}}{144}+\left\lfloor\frac{2 k}{N_{S A P}}\right\rfloor \tag{216}
\end{equation*}
$$
where

\begin{tabular}{ll} 
SAPreambleCarrierSet $_{n}$ & specifies all subcarriers allocated to the specific SA-Preamble. \\
$n$ & \begin{tabular}{l} 
is the index of the SA-Preamble carrier-set 0,1, and 2 representing \\
segment ID.
\end{tabular} \\
$k$ & is a running index 0 to $N_{S A P}-1$ for each FFT sizes.
\end{tabular}

No circular shift is assumed in Equation (216).

Each segment uses an SA-Preamble composed of a carrier-set out of the three available carrier-sets in the following manner:
- Segment 0 uses SA-Preamble carrier-set 0 .
- Segment 1 uses SA-Preamble carrier-set 1.
- Segment 2 uses SA-Preamble carrier-set 2.

Each cell ID has an integer value IDcell from 0 to 767. The IDcell is defined by segment index and an index per segment as follows:
$$
I \text { Dcell }=256 n+I d x
$$
where

\begin{tabular}{ll}
$n$ & is the index of the SA-Preamble carrier-set 0,1 and 2 \\
representing segment ID.
\end{tabular}

SA-Preamble sequences are partitioned, and each partition is dedicated to a specific base station type such as Macrocell ABS, Macro Hot-zone ABS, Femto ABS, or TTR Relay ABS. STR Relay ABS can be included in either the macro ABS partition or the Macro Hot-zone partition based on deployment. The base station types are categorized into macro ABS and non-macro ABS cells by hard partition with 258 sequences ( 86 sequences per segment $\times 3$ segments) dedicated for macro ABS. The non-macro ABS information is broadcasted in a hierarchical structure, which is composed of S-SFH SP3 and the AAI-SCD message. In SSFH SP3, non-macro ABS cell type is partitioned as public and CSG femto base stations. A total of 16 cases of IDcell partition for public and CSG-femto ABS are shown in Table 6-169, which is composed of IDcell partitions based on 30 sequence ( 10 sequences per segmentation) granularity.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-169-IDcell partitioning for public ABS and CSG-femto ABS}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Value indicated in 4bitSFH SP3 & IDcell partition for public ABS (Number of sequences per each segmentation) & IDcell partition for CSG-femto ABS (Number of sequences per each segmentation) \\
\hline 0000 & (0) & $86+256 \mathrm{n} \sim 255+256 \mathrm{n}(170)$ \\
\hline 0001 & $86+256 \mathrm{n} \sim 105+256 \mathrm{n}$ (20) & 106+256n ~255+256n (150) \\
\hline 0010 & $86+256 \mathrm{n} \sim 115+256$ n (30) & 116+256n ~255+256n (140) \\
\hline 0011 & $86+256 \mathrm{n} \sim 125+256 \mathrm{n}(40)$ & 126+256n ~255+256n (130) \\
\hline 0100 & $86+256$ n $\sim 135+256$ n (50) & 136+256n ~255+256n (120) \\
\hline 0101 & $86+256$ n $\sim 145+256$ n (60) & 146+256n ~255+256n (110) \\
\hline 0110 & $86+256 \mathrm{n} \sim 155+256 \mathrm{n}$ (70) & 156+256n ~255+256n (100) \\
\hline 0111 & $86+256 \mathrm{n} \sim 165+256 \mathrm{n}(80)$ & 166+256n ~255+256n (90) \\
\hline 1000 & $86+256 \mathrm{n} \sim 175+256 \mathrm{n}(90)$ & 176+256n ~255+256n (80) \\
\hline 1001 & $86+256 \mathrm{n} \sim 185+256 \mathrm{n}(100)$ & 186+256n ~255+256n (70) \\
\hline 1010 & $86+256 \mathrm{n} \sim 195+256 \mathrm{n}(110)$ & 196+256n ~255+256n (60) \\
\hline 1011 & $86+256 \mathrm{n} \sim 205+256 \mathrm{n}$ (120) & 206+256n ~255+256n (50) \\
\hline 1100 & $86+256 \mathrm{n} \sim 215+256 \mathrm{n}(130)$ & 216+256n ~255+256n (40) \\
\hline 1101 & $86+256 \mathrm{n} \sim 225+256 \mathrm{n}(140)$ & 226+256n ~255+256n (30) \\
\hline 1110 & $86+256 \mathrm{n} \sim 235+256 \mathrm{n}(150)$ & $236+256$ n $\sim 255+256$ n (20) \\
\hline 1111 & $86+256 \mathrm{n} \sim 255+256 \mathrm{n}$ (170) & (0) \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

For the support of femtocell deployment, a Femto ABS should self-configure the segment or subcarrier set for SA-Preamble transmission based on the segment information of the overlay macrocell ABS for minimized interference to macrocell if the Femto ABS is synchronized to macrocell ABSs. The segment information of the overlay macrocell ABS may be obtained by communications with macrocell ABS through backbone network or active scanning of SA-Preamble transmitted by macrocell ABS.

For the support of macro, public and CSG femto deployment, if $0 \leq I d x \leq 85$, it is reserved for macro ABS, if $86 \leq I d x \leq z$, it belongs to public ABS, and if $z+1 \leq I d x \leq 255$, it belongs to CSG femto ABS, where $z$ is calculated from SA-Preamble sequence soft partitioning information in S-SFH SP3 IE. The public ABS and CSG femto ABS may use different segments to the co-located macro ABS. CSG femto ABS may be further restricted to one segment to reduce interference to public ABS. Public ABS may choose a different segment to CSG femto ABS. For example, the public ABS may choose the segment as $i=\bmod ((n+1), 3)$, and CSG femto may choose the segment as $j=\bmod ((n-1), 3)$ if the co-located macro ABS uses segment $n$. The IDcell of public ABS may be $256 i+I d x, 86 \leq I d x \leq z$, and that of CSG femto ABS may be $256 j+I d x, z+1 \leq I d x \leq 255$. The public ABS also may choose the segment as $j=\bmod ((n-1), 3)$ to reduce interference between public ABSs.

The AAI-SCD message provides finer partition information. The non-macro public ABS category can be further divided into Macro hot-multicarrier zone, TTR Relay, and OSG femto. The CSG femto category can be further divided into CSG-closed and CSG-open.

AAI-SCD includes a 4-bit SA_PreamblePartitionforBS type indicator for each partition, which is ordered by the ABS type; macro hot-zone, TTR Relay, OSG femto, CSG-open femto, and CSG-closed femto ABSs. Each SA_PreamblePartitionforBStype indicates the last index of the preamble sequence of the corresponding ABS type as shown in Table 6-170. The range of Macro hot-zone starts from the beginning of the non-macro public ABS category. The range of CSG-open Femto starts from the beginning of the CSG femto ABS category. For all other ABS types within either the non-macro public ABS category or the CSG femto ABS category, the range of the preamble sequence of the corresponding ABS type starts right after the last index of the preamble sequence for the previous ABS type in the same category.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-170-IDcell partitioning}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|}
\hline Value indicated by SA_Preamble Partition for BS type in AAI-SCD & The last index of IDcell \\
\hline 0000 & 85+256n \\
\hline 0001 & 105+256n \\
\hline 0010 & 115+256n \\
\hline 0011 & 125+256n \\
\hline 0100 & 135+256n \\
\hline 0101 & 145+256n \\
\hline 0110 & 155+256n \\
\hline 0111 & 165+256n \\
\hline 1000 & 175+256n \\
\hline 1001 & 185+256n \\
\hline 1010 & 195+256n \\
\hline 1011 & 205+256n \\
\hline 1100 & 215+256n \\
\hline 1101 & 225+256n \\
\hline 1110 & 235+256n \\
\hline 1111 & 255+256n \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

For 512-FFT size, the 288-bit SA-Preamble sequence is divided into 8 main subblocks, namely, A, B, C, D, $\mathrm{E}, \mathrm{F}, \mathrm{G}$, and H . The length of each subblock is 36 bits. Each segment ID has different sequence subblocks. Table 6-171 to Table 6-173 depict the 8 subblocks of each segment ID where the sequence $\{+1,+\mathrm{j},-1,-\mathrm{j}\}$ for each subblock for each segment is represented in a QPSK manner. Table 6-171, Table 6-172, and Table 6-173 include 128 sequences indexed by $q$ from 0 to 127 in a hexadecimal format for segment 0 , segment 1 , and segment 2 , respectively. The modulation sequence is obtained by converting a hexadecimal $X_{i}^{(q)}$ of a subblock into two QPSK symbols $v^{(q)}{ }_{2 i}$ and $v_{2 i+1}^{(q)}$, where $i=0,1, \ldots, 7,8$. The converting equations are as follows:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& v_{2 i}^{(q)}=\exp \left(j \frac{\pi}{2}\left(2 \cdot b_{i, 0}^{(q)}+b_{i, 1}^{(q)} \quad\right)\right) \\
& v_{2 i+1}^{(q)}=\exp \left(j \frac{\pi}{2}\left(2 \cdot b_{i, 2}^{(q)}+b_{i, 3}^{(q)} \quad\right)\right)
\end{aligned}
$$
where
$$
X_{i}^{(q)}=2^{3} \cdot b_{i, 0}^{(q)}+2^{2} \cdot b_{i, 1}^{(q)}+2^{1} \cdot b_{i, 2}^{(q)}+2^{0} \cdot b_{i, 3}^{\ddot{q}}
$$

The other 128 sequences indexed by $q$ from 128 to 255 can be obtained by the following equation:
$$
v_{k}^{(q)}=\left(v_{k}^{(q-128)}\right)^{*}
$$
where
$$
q=128,129, \ldots, 254,255
$$

For 512-FFT size, A, B, C, D, E, F, G, and H are modulated and mapped sequentially in ascending order onto the circular-shifted SA-Preamble subcarrier-set corresponding to segment ID, as shown in Figure 6140. For higher FFT sizes, the basic subblocks (A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H) are repeated in the same order. For instance, in 1024-FFT size, E, F, G, H, A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H, A, B, C, D are modulated and mapped sequentially in ascending order onto the circular-shifted SA-Preamble subcarrier-set corresponding to segment ID.

\begin{figure}
\includegraphics[alt={},max width=\textwidth]{https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/b75642f5-8f88-4574-9a75-f32eadecb271-0635.jpg?height=420&width=1002&top_left_y=1839&top_left_x=567}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Figure 6-140-Allocation of sequence subblocks for each FFT}
\end{figure}

A circular shift is applied to more than three consecutive subcarriers after applying subcarrier mapping based on Equation (216). Each subblock has common offset. The circular shift pattern for each subblock is as follows:
$$
[2,1,0 \ldots, 2,1,0, \ldots, 2,1,0,2,1,0, \text { DC, } 1,0,2,1,0,2, \ldots, 1,0,2, \ldots, 1,0,2]
$$
where the shift is circularly right shift.
For 512-FFT size, the subblocks (A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H) experience the following right circular shift $(0,2,1,0,1,0,2,1)$, respectively. Figure 6-141 depicts the symbol structure of SA-Preamble in the frequency domain for 512-FFT.

\begin{figure}
\includegraphics[alt={},max width=\textwidth]{https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/b75642f5-8f88-4574-9a75-f32eadecb271-0636.jpg?height=549&width=1293&top_left_y=807&top_left_x=437}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Figure 6-141-SA-Preamble symbol structure for 512-FFT}
\end{figure}

For multiple antenna systems, the SA-Preamble blocks or subblocks are interleaved on the number of antennas as follows. For 512-FFT size, Figure 6-142 depicts the SA-Preamble allocation for 2, 4, and 8 antennas.

\begin{figure}
\includegraphics[alt={},max width=\textwidth]{https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/b75642f5-8f88-4574-9a75-f32eadecb271-0637.jpg?height=586&width=1107&top_left_y=330&top_left_x=481}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Figure 6-142-Multi-antenna example for 512-FFT}
\end{figure}

8 antennas: Each antenna sends one subblock $\{A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H\}$

For 1024-FFT size, Figure 6-143 depicts the SA-Preamble allocation for 2, 4, and 8 antennas.

\begin{figure}
\includegraphics[alt={},max width=\textwidth]{https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/b75642f5-8f88-4574-9a75-f32eadecb271-0637.jpg?height=877&width=1307&top_left_y=1231&top_left_x=311}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Figure 6-143-Multi-antenna example for 1024-FFT}
\end{figure}

For the 2048-FFT, Figure 6-144 depicts the SA-Preamble allocation for 2, 4, and 8 antennas.

\begin{figure}
\includegraphics[alt={},max width=\textwidth]{https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/b75642f5-8f88-4574-9a75-f32eadecb271-0638.jpg?height=753&width=1333&top_left_y=320&top_left_x=380}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Figure 6-144-Multi-antenna example for 2048-FFT}
\end{figure}

Let "block" denote 8 consecutive subblocks $\{\mathrm{E}, \mathrm{F}, \mathrm{G}, \mathrm{H}, \mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{D}\}$. For 512-FFT size, however, the positions of $\{\mathrm{E}, \mathrm{F}, \mathrm{G}, \mathrm{H}\}$ shall be swapped with $\{\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{D}\}$, as shown in Figure 6-142. The algorithm to assign the preamble blocks to multiple transmit antennas where the tone dropping is not applied and the number of antennas is power of 2 can be described as follows. Let
$N_{t}$ : number of transmit antennas
$N_{b}$ : total number of blocks
$N_{s}$ : total number of subblocks; $N_{s}=8 \times N_{b}$
$N_{b t}$ : number of blocks per antenna; $N_{b t}=N_{b} / N_{t}$
$N_{s t}$ : number of subblocks per antenna; $N_{s t}=N_{s} / N_{t}$

If ( $N_{b t} \geq 1$ )
Distribute consecutive blocks across the $N_{t}$ antennas
For a given antenna, a block is repeated with period $N_{t}$
Block position of the antenna $t$ is $t+p \times N_{t}$, where $t=0,1, \ldots, N_{t}-1, p=0,1, \ldots, N_{b t}-1$
Else
If ( $N_{s t}=4$ )
Interleave the 8 subblocks $\{\mathrm{E}, \mathrm{F}, \mathrm{G}, \mathrm{H}, \mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{D}\}$ across each 2 consecutive antennas
Block $[\mathrm{E}, 0, \mathrm{G}, 0, \mathrm{~A}, 0, \mathrm{C}, 0]$ is sent from antenna $i$ at block position: floor $(i / 2)$
Block $[0, \mathrm{~F}, 0, \mathrm{H}, 0, \mathrm{~B}, 0, \mathrm{D}]$ is sent from antenna $i+1$ at block position: floor $((i+1) / 2)$, where $i=0$ for 512-FFT, $i=0,2$ for 1024-FFT, and $i=0,2,4,6$ for 2048-FFT
Else If ( $N_{s t}=2$ )
Interleave the 8 subblocks $\{\mathrm{E}, \mathrm{F}, \mathrm{G}, \mathrm{H}, \mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{D}\}$ across each 4 consecutive antennas
Block $[\mathrm{E}, 0,0,0, \mathrm{~A}, 0,0,0]$ is sent from antenna $i$ at block position: floor $(i / 4)$
Block $[0,0, \mathrm{G}, 0,0,0, \mathrm{C}, 0]$ is sent from antenna $i+1$ at block position: floor $((i+1) / 4)$
Block $[0, \mathrm{~F}, 0,0,0, \mathrm{~B}, 0,0]$ is sent from antenna $i+2$ at block position: floor $((i+2) / 4)$

Block $[0,0,0, H, 0,0,0, D]$ is sent from antenna $i+3$ at block position: floor $((i+3) / 4)$, where $i=0$ for 512-FFT and $i=0,4$ for 1024-FFT

Else If ( $N_{s t}=1$ )
Interleave the 8 subblocks $\{\mathrm{E}, \mathrm{F}, \mathrm{G}, \mathrm{H}, \mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{D}\}$ across each 8 consecutive antennas; i.e., send 1 subblock per antenna

Block $[\mathrm{E}, 0,0,0,0,0,0,0]$ is sent from antenna $i$ at block position: floor $(i / 8)$
Block $[0, \mathrm{~F}, 0,0,0,0,0,0]$ is sent from antenna $i+1$ at block position: floor $((i+1) / 8)$
Block $[0,0, \mathrm{G}, 0,0,0,0,0]$ is sent from antenna $i+2$ at block position: floor $((i+2) / 8)$
Block $[0,0,0, \mathrm{H}, 0,0,0,0]$ is sent from antenna $i+3$ at block position: floor $((i+3) / 8)$
Block $[0,0,0,0, \mathrm{~A}, 0,0,0]$ is sent from antenna $i+4$ at block position: floor $((i+4) / 8)$
Block $[0,0,0,0,0, \mathrm{~B}, 0,0]$ is sent from antenna $i+5$ at block position: floor $((i+5) / 8)$
Block $[0,0,0,0,0,0, \mathrm{C}, 0]$ is sent from antenna $i+6$ at block position: floor $((i+6) / 8)$
Block $[0,0,0,0,0,0,0, \mathrm{D}]$ is sent from antenna $i+7$ at block position: floor $((i+7) / 8)$, where $i=0$ for 512-FFT.

At each time frame containing the SA-Preamble, the transmitted structures are rotated across the transmit antennas. For example, consider the 512 -FFT system with 4 transmit antennas. At the $f$-th frame, the SAPreamble structure $[\mathrm{A}, 0,0,0, \mathrm{E}, 0,0,0]$ is sent via the first antenna, and structure $[0,0,0, \mathrm{D}, 0,0,0, \mathrm{H}]$ is sent via the fourth antenna. In the ( $f+2$ )-th frame, structure $[0,0,0, \mathrm{D}, 0,0,0, \mathrm{H}]$ is sent via the first antenna, while structure $[\mathrm{A}, 0,0,0, \mathrm{E}, 0,0,0]$ is sent via the second antenna.

The magnitude boosting levels for different FFT sizes and number of antennas are shown in Table 6-174.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-174-SA-Preamble boosting levels}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline Ant|FFT & $\mathbf{5 1 2}$ & $\mathbf{1 k}$ & $\mathbf{2 k}$ \\
\hline 2 & 2.12 & 1.99 & 2.18 \\
\hline 4 & 3.50 & 2.91 & 3.22 \\
\hline 8 & 6.63 & 5.04 & 4.88 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

For two antennas and 512-FFT size case, the SA-Preamble is transmitted with a magnitude boost of 2.12 . The boosted SA-Preamble at the $k$-th subcarrier can be written as
$$
c_{k}=\frac{2.12}{\sqrt{N}_{t}} \times b_{k}
$$
where $b_{k}$ represents SA-Preamble before the boosting $(+1,-1,+\mathrm{j}$, or -j$)$ and $N_{t}$ is the number of transmit antennas. The block cover sequence shall be applied to each subblock. Each bit $\{0,1\}$ of block cover sequence shown in Table 6-175 as a hexadecimal format is mapped to real number $\{+1,-1\}$, and then multiplied to all the subcarriers in the corresponding subblock in the structure depicted in Figure 6-140.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-175-SA-Preamble block cover sequence}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline (FFT, number of antennas) Segment ID & 0 & 1 & 2 \\
\hline $(512,2)$ & 22 & 22 & 37 \\
\hline $(512,4)$ & 09 & 01 & 07 \\
\hline $(512,8)$ & 00 & 00 & 00 \\
\hline $(1024,2)$ & 7373 & 3030 & 0000 \\
\hline $(1024,4)$ & 3333 & 2D2D & 2727 \\
\hline $(1024,8)$ & 0F0F & 0404 & 0606 \\
\hline $(2048,2)$ & 7F55AA42 & 4216CC47 & 3A5A26D9 \\
\hline $(2048,4)$ & 6F73730E & 1F30305A & 77000013 \\
\hline $(2048,8)$ & 2F333319 & 0B2D2D03 & 0127271F \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

SA-Preamble sequences for tone dropping support are obtained by dropping the farthest subblocks of the reference bandwidth from the DC subcarrier on both sides.

For support of irregular nominal channel bandwidth by tone dropping, the dropping unit of SA-Preamble is two subblocks. The granularity of nominal channel bandwidth is 1.25 MHz . Table $6-176$ shows the allocation of sequence subblocks for SA-Preamble according to the different range of available nominal channel bandwidth, where $N_{s i}$ is the total number of subblocks in irregular nominal channel bandwidth.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-176-Allocation of sequence subblocks for tone dropping support}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Irregular nominal channel bandwidth range, x (MHz) & $N_{s i}$ & Allocation of sequence subblocks \\
\hline $5<\mathrm{x}<6.25$ & 8 & ABCD EFGH \\
\hline $6.25 \leq \mathrm{x}<7.5$ & 10 & H ABCD EFGH A \\
\hline $7.5 \leq \mathrm{x}<8.75$ & 12 & GH ABCD EFGH AB \\
\hline $8.75 \leq \mathrm{x}<10$ & 14 & FGH ABCD EFGH ABC \\
\hline $10<\mathrm{x}<11.25$ & 16 & EFGH ABCD EFGH ABCD \\
\hline $11.25 \leq \mathrm{x}<12.5$ & 18 & D EFGH ABCD EFGH ABCD E \\
\hline $12.5 \leq \mathrm{x}<13.75$ & 20 & CD EFGH ABCD EFGH ABCD EF \\
\hline $13.75 \leq \mathrm{x}<15$ & 22 & BCD EFGH ABCD EFGH ABCD EFG \\
\hline $15 \leq \mathrm{x}<16.25$ & 24 & ABCD EFGH ABCD EFGH ABCD EFGH \\
\hline $16.25 \leq \mathrm{x}<17.5$ & 26 & H ABCD EFGH ABCD EFGH ABCD EFGH A \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-176-Allocation of sequence subblocks for tone dropping support (continued)}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Irregular nominal channel bandwidth range, x (MHz) & $N_{s i}$ & Allocation of sequence subblocks \\
\hline $17.5 \leq \mathrm{x}<18.75$ & 28 & GH ABCD EFGH ABCD EFGH ABCD EFGH AB \\
\hline $18.75 \leq \mathrm{x}<20$ & 30 & FGH ABCD EFGH ABCD EFGH ABCD EFGH ABC \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

In case of $5<\mathrm{x}<6.25$, the antenna configuration of $512-\mathrm{FFT}$ size for the regular channel bandwidth 5 MHz is used. In case of $10<\mathrm{x}<11.25$, the antenna configuration of 1024-FFT size for the regular channel bandwidth 10 MHz is used. Except for these two cases, the algorithm to assign the SA-Preamble subblocks to multiple transmit antennas in the irregular system bandwidth is described as follows. Let $N_{s t, k}$ be the number of subblocks for $k$-th antenna, where $k=0,1, \ldots, N_{t}-1$. Then $N_{s t, k}$ is defined by Equation (217).
$$
\begin{equation*}
N_{s t, k}=\left\lfloor\frac{N_{s i}+N_{t}-1-\bmod \left(k-p, N_{t}\right)}{N_{t}}\right\rfloor \tag{217}
\end{equation*}
$$
where
$$
p=\left\lfloor\frac{N_{t}-\bmod \left(N_{s i}, N_{t}\right)}{2}\right\rfloor
$$

The subblock position for the $k$-th antenna is $\left(t+\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} N_{s t, i}\right) \ldots$, where $t=0,1, \ldots, N_{s t, k}-1$, and $N_{s t,-1}=0$.
Figure 6-145 shows the proposed SA-Preamble allocation at $11.25 \leq x<12.5$ as an example.

\begin{figure}
\includegraphics[alt={},max width=\textwidth]{https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/b75642f5-8f88-4574-9a75-f32eadecb271-0642.jpg?height=1414&width=1302&top_left_y=318&top_left_x=319}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Figure 6-145-Proposed allocation of SA-Preamble at $11.25 \leq x<12.5$}
\end{figure}

At each time frame containing SA-Preamble, the transmit structures are rotated across the transmit antennas. The rotating direction is same as the reference channel bandwidth.

The block cover sequence shall be applied to each subblock. Each bit $\{0,1\}$ of block cover sequence shown in Table 6-178 as a hexadecimal format is mapped to real number $\{+1,-1\}$, and then multiplied to all the subcarriers in the subblock structure corresponding to the tone dropping design. In Table 6-178, $B W_{\text {min }}$ denotes the minimum value (MHz) of Irregular Nominal Channel Bandwidth Range as shown in Table 6138. BCS for the range $5<x<6.25$ is the same as BCS of 512-FFT size for the regular channel bandwidth 5 MHz , and BCS for the range $10<x<11.25$ is the same as BCS of 1024-FFT size for the regular channel bandwidth 10 MHz as shown in Table 6-175.

The magnitude boosting levels for different $B W_{\text {min }}$ and number of antennas are shown in Table 6-177.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-177-SA-Preamble boosting levels for tone dropping support}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline $B W_{\text {min }} \backslash$ Ant & 2 & 4 & 8 \\
\hline 6.25 & 2.22 & 3.09 & 4.26 \\
\hline 7.5 & 2.27 & 3.54 & 4.92 \\
\hline 8.75 & 2.20 & 3.06 & 5.24 \\
\hline 11.25 & 2.34 & 3.04 & 5.18 \\
\hline 12.5 & 2.43 & 3.31 & 5.66 \\
\hline 13.75 & 2.44 & 3.08 & 5.83 \\
\hline 15 & 2.52 & 3.33 & 6.32 \\
\hline 16.25 & 2.59 & 3.16 & 5.39 \\
\hline 17.5 & 2.71 & 3.38 & 5.78 \\
\hline 18.75 & 2.66 & 3.26 & 5.94 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}
