\subsection*{6.3.8.3.1.6 Operation of EDI for request for switching MFM}

The 57th codeword in PFBCH is defined for transition of MFM between distributed and localized permutations, where MFM $0,1,4$, and 7 are corresponding to the distributed permutation, and MFM 2, 3, 5, and 6 are corresponding to the localized permutation. Based on the measurements such as the channel variation, the speed of the AMS, and so on, the AMS triggers transition to the appropriate permutation by transmitting the codeword. The transmission of the codeword means the request from an AMS for transition to another permutation from the current permutation corresponding to MFM, which was assigned by Feedback allocation A-MAP IE. If PFBCH is being used according to the feedback format, the AMS transmits the codeword at any feedback opportunity. If SFBCH is being used according to the feedback format, the AMS transmits SFBCH with setting the 'PFBCH indicator' to '1' first, and then transmits the codeword through PFBCH in the next feedback opportunity. When the ABS receives the codeword, the ABS may send Feedback allocation A-MAP IE to the AMS to assign the appropriate MFM that the ABS determines based on the request from the AMS. In order to determine the appropriate MFM, the ABS may transmit Feedback polling A-MAP IE to the AMS to obtain more MIMO feedback information before sending Feedback allocation A-MAP IE. When the ABS does not send the Feedback allocation A-MAP IE and/or the AMS does not receive the Feedback allocation A-MAP IE, the AMS shall keep reporting the feedback information. When the ABS sends the Feedback allocation A-MAP IE and the AMS receives the Feedback allocation A-MAP IE, the previous fast feedback channel is automatically deallocated and the AMS shall report the new feedback information through the newly allocated fast feedback channel.

\subsection*{6.3.8.3.2 Bandwidth request channel}

The quick access message contains a 12 -bit MSID and 4-bit predefined BR information defined in 6.2.11.1.5.1.

\subsection*{6.3.8.4 Uplink power control}

Uplink power control is supported for both an initial calibration and a periodic adjustment on transmit power without loss of data. The uplink power control algorithm determines the transmission power of an OFDM symbol to compensate for the pathloss, shadowing, and fast fading. Uplink power control shall intend to control inter-cell interference level.

A transmitting AMS shall maintain the transmitted power density, unless the maximum power level is reached. In other words, when the number of active LRUs allocated to a user is reduced, the total transmitted power shall be reduced proportionally by the AMS, as long as there is no additional change of parameters for power control. When the number of LRU is increased, the total transmitted power shall also be increased proportionally. However, the transmitted power level shall not exceed the maximum levels dictated by signal integrity considerations and regulatory requirements.

An AMS shall report the maximum available power of the carrier for initial network entry by MAX Tx Power field when the AMS transmits AAI-SBC-REQ, and shall report the maximum power of available carriers when the AMS sends AAI-MC-REQ.

For interference level control, the current interference level of each ABS may be shared among ABSs.

The power per subcarrier and per stream shall be calculated according to Equation (302).
$$
\begin{equation*}
P(d B m)=L+S I N R_{T \arg e t}+N I+O f f s e t \tag{302}
\end{equation*}
$$
where
SINR $_{\text {Target }}$ is the target uplink SINR received by the ABS.

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|}
\hline P & is the Tx power level (dBm) per stream and per subcarrier for the current transmission. \\
\hline L & is the estimated average current DL propagation loss calculated by the AMS. It shall include the AMS's Tx antenna gain and path loss. \\
\hline NI & is the estimated average power level $(\mathrm{dBm})$ of the noise and interference per subcarrier at the ABS, as indicated in the AAI-ULPC-NI message. \\
\hline Offset & is a correction term for the AMS-specific power offset. It is controlled by the ABS through power control messages. There are two kinds of Offset values that are used for data and control, respectively. Further details are given in the following subclauses (6.3.8.4.1, 6.3.8.4.2, and 6.3.8.4.3). \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

The estimated average current DL propagation loss, $L$, shall be calculated based on the total power received on the active subcarriers of the frame preamble.

\subsection*{6.3.8.4.1 Power control for data channel}

For data channel transmission, the $\operatorname{SINR}_{\text {Target }}$ term in Equation (302) is calculated according to Equation (303).
$$
\begin{equation*}
S I N R_{T \arg e t}=10 \log 10\left(\max \left(10^{\Lambda}\left(\frac{S I N R_{M I N}(d B)}{10}\right), \gamma_{I o T} \times S I R_{D L}-\alpha\right)\right)-\beta \times 10 \log 10(T N S) \tag{303}
\end{equation*}
$$
where Equation (303) is the target SINR value for IoT control and tradeoff between overall system throughput and cell edge performance, decided by the control parameter $\gamma_{\text {IoT }}$ and $\operatorname{SINR}_{\text {MIN }}(\mathrm{dB})$. The parameters used in Equation (303) are broadcasted in the AAI-SCD message unless otherwise noted. The explanation for the parameters is as follows:

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|}
\hline - $\operatorname{SINR}_{\text {MIN }}(\mathrm{dB})$ & is the SINR requirement (dataSinrMin) for the minimum rate expected by the ABS . \\
\hline $-\gamma_{\text {IoT }}$ & is the fairness and IoT control factor broadcasted. \\
\hline - $\operatorname{SIR}_{D L}$ & is the linear ratio of the downlink signal to interference power, measured by the AMS. \\
\hline $-\alpha$ & is the factor according to the number of receive antennas at the ABS . \\
\hline $-\beta$ & is set to be zero or one to determine the influence of TNS on SINR ${ }_{\text {Target }} \cdot \cdot$ \\
\hline - TNS & is the Total Number of Streams in the LRU indicated by UL A-MAP IE. In case of SU-MIMO, this value shall be set to $M_{t}$ where $M_{t}$ is the number of streams for the AMS. In case of CSM, TNS is the aggregated number of streams. \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

When calculated data channel $\operatorname{SINR}_{\text {Target }}$ is higher than dataSinrMax defined in the AAI-SCD message, SINR $_{\text {Target }}$ shall be set to dataSinrMax.

For a data channel transmission, the Offset in Equation (302) shall be set to the value Offset $_{\text {Data }}$ conveyed in an AAI-UL_POWER_ADJ message. The value used shall be taken from the most recent message that preceded the first uplink subframe of the frame by more than $T_{D L \_R x \_P r o c e s s i n g}$. An AMS shall set initial Offset $_{\text {Data }}$ as Offset $_{\text {Initial }}$ after the AMS succeeds for initial ranging process. Here Offset $_{\text {Initial }}$ is calculated by an AMS during initial ranging process according to 6.3.8.4.4.

\subsection*{6.3.8.4.2 Power control for control channels}

In the case of control channel transmission, except for initial ranging and sounding transmission, $\operatorname{SINR}_{\text {Target }}$ in Equation (302) is set according to Table 6-290. The parameters in Table 6-290 are conveyed in the AAISCD message.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-290-SINR ${ }_{\text {Target }}$ parameters for control channels}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|}
\hline Control channel type & $S I N R_{\text {Target }}$ parameters \\
\hline HARQ Feedback & targetHarqSinr \\
\hline Synchronized Ranging & targetSyncRangingSinr \\
\hline P-FBCH & targetPfbchSinr \\
\hline \multirow{2}{*}{S-FBCH} & targetSfbchBaseSinr \\
\hline & targetSfbchDeltaSinr \\
\hline Bandwidth Request & targetBwRequestSinr \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

For HARQ Feedback, Synchronized Ranging, P-FBCH and Bandwidth Request, the $S I N R_{\text {Target }}$ values are indicated directly in Table 6-290. For the S-FBCH channel, the $S I N R_{\text {Target }}$ value is defined in Equation (304).
$$
\begin{equation*}
S I N R_{T \arg e t}(S F B C H)=S F B C H_{\text {Base }}+\left(l-l_{\min }\right) \times S F B C H_{\text {Delta }} \tag{304}
\end{equation*}
$$
where

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|}
\hline $S F B C H_{\text {Base }}$ & is base SINR $_{\text {Target }}$ value signaled by targetSfbchBaseSinr. \\
\hline $S F B C H_{\text {Delta }}$ & is differential SINR $_{\text {Target }}$ value signaled by targetSfbchDeltaSinr. \\
\hline $l$ & is S-FBCH payload information bits number defined in 6.3.8.2.1.2. \\
\hline $l_{\text {min }}$ & is the minimum S-FBCH payload information bits number ( $l_{\text {min }}=7$ ) defined in 6.3.8.2.1.2. \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

For a control channel transmission, the Offset in Equation (302) shall be set to the value Offset $_{\text {Control }}$ conveyed as offsetControl in an AAI-UL_POWER_ADJ message. The value used shall be taken from the most recent message that preceded the first uplink subframe of the frame by more than $T_{D L \_R x \_ \text {Processing }}$. An AMS shall set initial Offset $_{\text {Control }}$ as Offset $_{\text {Initial }}+$ offsetControl after the AMS succeeds for initial ranging process. Here Offset $_{\text {Initial }}$ is calculated by an AMS during initial ranging process according to 6.3.8.4.4 and offsetControl is included in CDMA Allocation A-MAP IE (6.3.8.4.8).

If an AMS does not receive any BR-ACK A-MAP IE at the ( $\left.n+B R \_A C K \_O f f s e t\right)$-th frame who transmitted a Bandwidth Request preamble sequence at the $n$-th frame, the AMS considers it as an implicit-NACK and may restart BR procedure. If it has continuously failed to receive BR-ACK A-MAP IE with $N$ time(s) and if it restarts the $(N+1)$-th BR procedure, it shall increase Tx Power by $N \times P_{I R, \text { Step }} \mathrm{dB}$ from the BR preamble transmission power which is decided by Equation (302), where $P_{I R, S t e p}$ is the step size to ramp up, which is 2 dB .

\subsection*{6.3.8.4.3 Power correction using PC-A-MAP}

The ABS may change the AMS's Tx power through direct power adjustment by PC-A-MAP. When the AMS receives its PC-A-MAP IE from the ABS, it shall modify its Offset $_{\text {Control }}$ value according to Equation (305).
$$
\begin{equation*}
\text { Offset }_{\text {Control }}=\text { Offset }_{\text {Control }}+\Delta_{\text {PowerAdjust }} \tag{305}
\end{equation*}
$$
where $\Delta_{\text {PowerAdjust }}$ is the power correction value indicated by the ABS through PC-A-MAP.

If the AMS receives the AAI-UL_POWER_ADJUST message at $i$-th frame, the AMS's Offset ${ }_{\text {Control }}$ shall be replaced by offsetControl indicated by the message from the ( $i+1$ )-th frame. Also PC-A-MAP IE from ( $i+1$ )-th frame is effective on this new value.

\subsection*{6.3.8.4.4 Initial ranging channel power control}

For initial ranging, the AMS sends an initial ranging code at a randomly selected ranging channel. The initial transmission power is decided according to measured RSS. If the AMS does not receive a response, it may increase its power level by $P_{\mathrm{IR}, \text { Step }}$ and may send a new initial ranging code, where $P_{\mathrm{IR}, \text { Step }}$ is the step size to ramp up, which is 2 dB . The AMS could further increase the power until maximum transmit power is reached.

The initial transmission power of the AMS is calculated as shown in Equation (306).
$$
\begin{equation*}
P_{\text {TX_IR_MIN }}=E I R x P_{\text {IR, min }}+B S_{-} E I R P-R S S \tag{306}
\end{equation*}
$$
where $E I R x P_{I R, \text { min }}$ is the minimum targeting receiving power and $B S \_E I R P$ is the transmission power of the BS, which are obtained from S-SFH SP2 and SP1, respectively, and RSS is the received signal strength measured by the AMS.

In the case that the Rx and Tx gain of the AMS antenna are different, the AMS shall use Equation (307).
$$
\begin{equation*}
P_{T X_{-} I R_{-} M I N}=E I R x P_{I R, \min }+B S_{-} E I R P-R S S+\left(G_{R x_{-} M S}-G_{T x_{-} M S}\right) \tag{307}
\end{equation*}
$$
where $G_{R x \_M S}$ is the antenna gain of the AMS Rx, and $G_{T x \_M S}$ is the antenna gain of the AMS Tx.
If the AMS receives RNG-ACK for continue mode without power adjustment (power level adjustment indication bit equals 0 ) as response to the corresponding ranging code transmitted by the AMS, the AMS shall use its latest ranging transmission power as the initial power level for next continued ranging transmission. The power ramping up will be performed for the next ranging transmission if there is no response within the given time.

If RNG-ACK has a power level adjustment indication bit with 1 , transmission power for continue mode shall be based on the power used for the latest ranging and the AMS shall adjust it by the amount of power level adjustment field of RNG-ACK. The adjusted power level will be used as the power level for next continued ranging transmission, and the power ramping up will be performed for the next ranging transmission if there is no response within the given time.

After an AMS completes the initial ranging process successfully with $N$ times ramping up and $M$ times RNG-ACK, initial offset value Offset $_{\text {Initial }}$ is determined as Equation (308).
$$
\begin{align*}
& \text { Offset }_{\text {Initial }}=P_{T X_{-} I R_{-} \text {Final }}-\left(L+\text { SINR }_{\text {InitialRanging }}+N I\right)  \tag{308}\\
& -10 \log 10(\text { RangingSubcarrierNum })
\end{align*}
$$
where
$$
\begin{array}{ll}
L \text { and } N I & \text { are defined in 6.3.8.4. } \\
\operatorname{SINR}_{\text {InitialRanging }} & \text { is defined as Equation (309). } \\
\operatorname{SINR}_{\text {InitialRanging }}= & \text { offsetControl }+t \arg \text { etInitialRangingSinr } \tag{309}
\end{array}
$$
where
offsetControl
is included in CDMA Allocation A-MAP IE (6.3.8.4.8).

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|}
\hline targetInitialRangingSinr & is the default initial ranging target SINR defined in Table 6-291. \\
\hline RangingSubcarrierNum & is the number of initial ranging subcarrier number, as 72 defined in 6.3.8.1.4.1. \\
\hline $P_{\text {TX_IR_Final }}$ & is the final initial ranging transmission power defined in Equation (310). \\
\hline \multicolumn{2}{|c|}{$P_{\text {TX_IR_Final }}=P_{\text {TX_IR_MIN }}+N \times P_{\text {IR, Step }}+\sum P_{\text {RNG-ACK }}(m)$} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
where $P_{\text {RNG-ACK }}(m)$ is the power level adjustment value of the $m$-th received RNG-ACK message ( $m=1, \ldots$, $M$ ) during the ranging process, and $P_{I R, \text { Step }}$ is the step size to ramp up, which is 2 dB .

Offset $_{\text {Initial }}$ shall be included in the AAI-RNG-REQ message to be sent to the serving the ABS right after the initial ranging process.

\subsection*{6.3.8.4.5 Sounding channel power control}

Power control for the UL sounding channel is supported to manage the sounding quality. The AMS's transmit power for UL sounding channel is controlled separately according to its sounding channel target SINR value. The transmission power per subcarrier shall be determined by multiplying the modulation coefficient $b_{k}$, calculated as per Equation (289) or Equation (290), by the linear scale factor corresponding to $P(d B m)$ derived from Equation (302).

In Equation (302), SINR $_{\text {Target }}$ is the sounding channel target SINR, which is set according to the DL SIR of the AMS defined by parameter $S I R_{D L}$. In order to maintain the UL sounding quality, the different target SINR values are assigned according to the DL SIR of each AMS; the AMS with high DL SIR applies relatively high target SINR, and the AMS with low DL SIR applies relatively low target SINR.

The $\operatorname{SINR}_{\text {Target }}$ for sounding channel shall be calculated from Equation (302) using the following parameter settings: SINR ${ }_{\text {MIN }}$ is the minimum SINR requirement expected by the ABS that is set to soundingSinrMin, $\gamma_{I o T}$ is set to the IoT control factor for sounding channel gammaIotSounding, SIR $_{D L}$ is the ratio of the downlink signal versus interference power, measured by the AMS, $\alpha$ is set to $0, \beta$ is set to 0 or 1 by the AAI-SCD message, TNS is set to 1 when antenna switching field equals 0 , and TNS is set to the number of Tx antenna when antenna switching field equals 1 .

When calculated sounding $\operatorname{SINR}_{\text {Target }}$ is higher than soundingSinrMax, the $\operatorname{SINR}_{\text {Target }}$ shall be set to soundingSinrMax.

Offset for sounding channel in Equation (302) is set to 0 .

If power boosting field in UL Sounding Command A-MAP IE is set $1,3 \mathrm{~dB}$ tone power boosting is applied to each Tx antenna.

All parameters necessary for sounding channel power control are transmitted through the AAI-SCD message in 6.2.3.30.

\subsection*{6.3.8.4.6 Concurrent transmission of uplink control channel and data}

In case of simultaneous transmission among control channels, uplink transmission power of assigned channels is determined based on the channel list in Table 6-291 in descending order until the total transmission power reaches maximum power limitation of the AMS.

If the AMS has not sufficient power to transmit an assigned channel, compared to the power level determined by Equation (302), then the AMS shall transmit this channel with the remaining power, even if it is insufficient to meet the requirements of Equation (302).

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-291-Priority of uplink transmit channels}
\begin{tabular}{|l|}
\hline \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Channel type } \\
\hline HARQ feedback \\
\hline PFBCH/SFBCH \\
\hline Synchronized Ranging \\
\hline Sounding \\
\hline DATA \\
\hline Bandwidth Request \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\subsection*{6.3.8.4.7 Uplink power status reporting}

\subsection*{6.3.8.4.7.1 Power status reporting information}

There are two key parameters for power status reporting: the base uplink transmission PSD and the $\operatorname{SIR}_{D L}$ defined in 6.3.8.4.1.

The base uplink transmission PSD is derived from Equation (302) by setting $\operatorname{SINR}_{\text {Target }}=0$ and Offset $=0$ :
$$
\begin{equation*}
P S D(\text { base })=L+N I \tag{311}
\end{equation*}
$$

The parameter is reported in dBm and is coded using 8 bits in 0.5 dBm steps ranging from -74 dBm (coded 0x00) to 53.5 dBm (coded 0 xFF ). The NI value used here is the most recent NI value of the frequency partition with UL control channel.

The reported $S I R_{D L}$ value is coded using 10 bits in $1 / 16 \mathrm{~dB}$ steps ranging from -12 dB (coded 0 x 000 ) to 51.9375 dB (coded 0x3ff).

After the ABS has received $P S D$ (base) and $\operatorname{SIR}_{D L}$ from the AMS, the PSD for all uplink channels can be estimated in the ABS.

\subsection*{6.3.8.4.7.2 Status transmission condition}

The AMS shall send the status reporting message Uplink Power Status Report header (defined in 6.2.2.1.3.6) to the ABS when the conditions are as listed in Equation (312) through Equation (314).
$$
\begin{equation*}
\left|M\left(n_{\text {last }}\right)-M(n)\right| \geq \text { txPowerReportThreshold } \tag{312}
\end{equation*}
$$
and
$$
\begin{equation*}
n-n_{\text {last }} \geq \text { txPowerReportMinimumInterval } \tag{313}
\end{equation*}
$$
or
$$
\begin{equation*}
n-n_{\text {last }} \geq \text { txPowerReportPeriodicalInterval } \tag{314}
\end{equation*}
$$
where $M$ is defined in Equation (315):
$$
\begin{array}{ll}
M=L+S I N R_{\text {Target }} \text { (Reported) } &  \tag{315}\\
L & \text { is the pathloss defined in Equation (302). } \\
S I N R_{\text {Target }} & \text { is defined in Equation (303) with beta }=0 \text { and } S I N R_{\text {Target }} \text { (Reported) is the } \\
& \text { value measured at the moment of 'Reported'. } \\
n_{\text {last }} & \text { is the frame when the last AMS status reporting message was sent. } \\
n & \text { is the current frame. }
\end{array}
$$

The status reporting configuration parameters txPowerReportThreshold, txPowerReportMinimumInterval, and txPowerReportPeriodicalInterval are sent from the ABS to the AMS in the AAI-UL_PSR_CFG message (defined in 6.2.3.34).

The AMS shall send the first power status report right after the AMS receives the first AAI-UL_PSR_CFG message with uplinkPowerStatusReport $=1$.

\subsection*{6.3.8.4.8 Uplink power control in initial network entry and reentry}

During initial network entry and reentry, after a successful initial ranging process, the ABS needs to send CDMA Allocation A-MAP IE to the AMS as shown in Figure 6-73.

The key power control parameters NI and offsetControl, which are signaled by CDMA Allocation A-MAP IE, directly belong to the FP of assigned data burst when multi-FPs are used.

When the AMS has only successfully received the CDMA Allocation A-MAP IE, the AMS will set NI and offsetControl by the values from CDMA Allocation A-MAP IE. Other uplink power control parameters are set by the default value listed in Table 6-292.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-292-Default ULPC parameters}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Type & Parameters & Default value \\
\hline \multirow[t]{5}{*}{Data Channel Control Parameters} & Alpha & 1/2 \\
\hline & Beta & 0 \\
\hline & Gamma & 0.5 \\
\hline & dataSinrMin & 3 dB \\
\hline & dataSinrMax & 40 dB \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-292-Default ULPC parameters (continued)}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Type & Parameters & Default value \\
\hline \multirow[t]{7}{*}{Control Channel Target SINR Value} & targetHarqSinr & 2 dB \\
\hline & targetInitialRangingSinr & 0 dB \\
\hline & targetSyncRangingSinr & $-3 \mathrm{~dB}$ \\
\hline & targetPfbchSinr & 0 dB \\
\hline & targetSfbchBaseSinr & 1.5 dB \\
\hline & targetSfbchDeltaSinr & 0.24 dB \\
\hline & targetBwRequestSinr & 0.5 dB \\
\hline Offset Initialization & offsetData & 0 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

Whenever the AMS successfully receives AAI-SCD, AAI-ULPC-NI, and AAI-UL_POWER_ADJ messages, the signaled uplink power control parameters will be updated accordingly.

\subsection*{6.3.8.4.9 Uplink power control in handover}

During the handover processing, if CDMA_RNG_FLAG in the AAI-HO-CMD message is set to 0 , CDMAbased ranging will be skipped and the power offset values for the T-ABS will be included in the AAI-HOCMD, as shown in Table 6-40. Other uplink power control parameters for the T-ABS are set by using the SABS value.

If CDMA_RNG_FLAG in the AAI-HO-CMD message is set to 1 and dedicated CDMA ranging code is not present, CDMA based ranging shall be performed, and the power control parameters of the T-ABS will be determined using the process defined in 6.3.8.4.8.

Before the handover procedure has been finished, two sets of uplink power control parameters are kept in the AMS to support uplink transmission to the S-ABS and the T-ABS separately.

If CDMA_RNG_FLAG in the AAI-HO-CMD message is set to 1 and dedicated CDMA ranging code is present, CDMA based ranging shall be performed, and iotFP and offsetControl for the T-ABS will be included in the AAI-HO-CMD, as shown in Table 6-34. In this case, the AMS sets iotFP and offsetControl by the values from AAI-HO-CMD. All other power control parameters of the T-ABS are determined according to 6.3.8.4.8

After handover, whenever the AMS successfully receives AAI-SCD, AAI-ULPC-NI, and AAI-UL-POWER-ADJ messages from the T-ABS, the signaled uplink power control parameters will be updated accordingly.

In case of zone switch from LZone to MZone, power control parameters (i.e., iotFP, offsetControl) for the MZone of the S-ABS shall be included in the Zone Switch TLV if TSTID is also included. In such a case, CDMA-based ranging will be skipped and the AMS will perform a bandwidth request procedure in MZone using the power offset values included in the Zone Switch TLV. Other uplink power control parameters for the T-ABS are set by using the S-ABS value.

\subsection*{6.3.8.4.10 Uplink power control in multicarrier operation}

In multicarrier operation mode, the pathloss reference of uplink power control for a given carrier shall be the same (downlink) carrier in case of TDD mode. In FDD mode, the pathloss reference shall be the paired downlink carrier.

\subsection*{6.3.8.5 Uplink physical structure for multicarrier support}

Guard subcarriers between carriers form integer multiples of PRUs. The structure of guard PRU is the same as the structure defined in 6.3.7.1. The guard PRUs are used as miniband CRUs at partition $\mathrm{FP}_{0}$ for data transmission only. The number of usable guard subcarriers is predefined and should be known to both the AMS and the ABS based on carrier bandwidth.

\subsection*{6.3.9 Uplink MIMO}

\subsection*{6.3.9.1 Uplink MIMO architecture and data processing}

The architecture of uplink MIMO at the transmitter side is shown in Figure 6-197.

\begin{figure}
\includegraphics[alt={},max width=\textwidth]{https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/b75642f5-8f88-4574-9a75-f32eadecb271-0864.jpg?height=428&width=1281&top_left_y=1052&top_left_x=415}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Figure 6-197-UL MIMO architecture}
\end{figure}

The MIMO encoder block maps a single MIMO layer ( $L=1$ ) onto $M_{t}\left(M_{t} \geq L\right)$ MIMO streams, which are fed to the Precoder block.

For SU-MIMO and collaborative spatial multiplexing (MU-MIMO), only one channel coding block exists in the allocated RU (vertical MIMO encoding at transmit side).

The precoder block maps MIMO stream(s) to antennas by generating the antenna-specific data symbols according to the selected MIMO mode.

The MIMO encoder and precoder blocks shall be omitted when the AMS has one transmit antenna.
The subcarrier mapping blocks map antenna-specific data to the OFDM symbol.

\subsection*{6.3.9.1.1 MIMO layer to MIMO stream mapping}

MIMO layer to MIMO stream mapping is performed by the MIMO encoder. The uplink MIMO encoder is identical to the downlink MIMO encoder described in 6.3.6.1.1 but with only a single MIMO layer ( $L=1$ ).

Multilayer encoding ( $\mathrm{MEF}=0 \mathrm{~b} 10$ ) is not supported for uplink transmissions. Collaborative spatial multiplexing (CSM) is achieved with vertical encoding (MEF $=0 \mathrm{~b} 01$ ) at the AMS. The STC rate per AMS for uplink SU-MIMO and MU-MIMO (CSM) transmissions is defined as $R=M_{t} / N_{F}$.

An AMS with 1 transmit antenna shall use vertical encoding ( $\mathrm{MEF}=0 \mathrm{~b} 01$ ) for uplink transmissions.

\subsection*{6.3.9.1.1.1 SFBC encoding}

Uplink SFBC encoding is identical to the downlink SFBC encoding described in 6.3.6.1.1.1.

SFBC encoding format shall not be allocated to an AMS with 1 transmit antenna.

\subsection*{6.3.9.1.1.2 Vertical encoding}

Uplink vertical encoding is identical to the downlink vertical encoding described in 6.3.6.1.1.2.

Vertical encoding with 1 MIMO stream ( $M_{t}=1$ ) format shall be allocated to an AMS with 1 transmit antenna.

\subsection*{6.3.9.1.2 MIMO stream to antenna mapping}

MIMO stream to antenna mapping is performed by the precoder. The uplink mapping is identical to the downlink mapping described in 6.3.6.1.2.

\subsection*{6.3.9.1.2.1 Non-adaptive precoding}

There is no precoding if there is only one transmit antenna at the MS.

With non-adaptive precoding, the precoding matrix is an $N_{t} \times M_{t}$ matrix $\mathbf{W}(k)$, where $N_{t}$ is the number of transmit antennas, $M_{t}$ is the numbers of MIMO streams, and $k$ is the physical index of the subcarrier where $\mathbf{W}(k)$ is applied. The matrix $\mathbf{W}$ is selected from a subset of size $N_{w}$ precoders of the base codebook for a given rank. $\mathbf{W}$ belongs to one of the subsets of the base codebook specified in 6.3.9.4.1 or the base codebook in 6.3.9.3.1.1 and 6.3.9.3.2.1, according to the type of allocation, MEF, $N_{t}$ and $M_{t}$, as specified in Table 6-293 and Table 6-294.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-293-Codebook subsets used for non-adaptive precoding in UL DLRU and NLRU}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|}
\hline MEF & RU with $M_{t}$ pilot streams \\
\hline SFBC & $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{UL}, \mathrm{OL}, \mathrm{SU}}\left(N_{t}, M_{t}, N_{w}\right), M_{t}=2$ \\
\hline VE & \begin{tabular}{l}
$\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{UL}, \mathrm{OL}, \mathrm{SU}}\left(N_{t}, M_{t}, N_{w}\right), M_{t}=1, \ldots, 4$ \\
$N_{w}$ depends on $M_{t}$
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The notation $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{UL}, \mathrm{OL}, \mathrm{SU}}\left(N_{t}, M_{t}, N_{w}\right)$ and the values of $N_{w}$ for the open-loop codebook subsets are defined in 6.3.9.4.1.

The notation $\mathrm{C}_{\text {base, } \mathrm{SU}}\left(N_{t}, M_{t}, N B\right)$ for the base codebooks is defined in 6.3.9.3.
In a RU allocated in a AAI subframe with MEF $=0 \mathrm{~b} 00$ (SFBC) or 0 b 01 (VE) and non-adaptive precoding, the matrix $\mathbf{W}$ changes every $N_{1} P_{S C}$ contiguous physical subcarriers according to Equation (316), and it does not depend on the AAI subframe number. When supporting FDM based UL PUSC zone, the matrix W for non-adaptive precoding changes every $N_{1} P_{S C}$ contiguous physical subcarriers that start from physical subcarrier index $=0$. The $N_{t} \times M_{t}$ precoding matrix $\mathbf{W}(k)$ applied on subcarrier $k$ in physical subband $s$ is selected as the codeword of index $i$ in the open-loop codebook subset of rank $M_{t}$, where $i$ is given by Equation (316).

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-294-Codebook subsets used for non-adaptive precoding in UL SLRU}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|}
\hline MEF & RU with $M_{t}$ pilot streams \\
\hline SFBC & $$
\begin{aligned}
& N_{t}=2: \mathrm{C}_{\text {base,UL }}(2,2,3) \\
& N_{t}=4: \mathrm{C}_{\text {base,UL }}(4,2,6)
\end{aligned}
$$ \\
\hline VE & $$
\begin{aligned}
& N_{t}=2: \mathrm{C}_{\text {base }, \mathrm{UL}}(2,1,4) \text { if } M_{t}=1 \text { or } \mathrm{C}_{\text {base }, \mathrm{UL}}(2,2,3) \text { if } \\
& M_{t}=2 \\
& N_{t}=4: \mathrm{C}_{\text {base }, \mathrm{UL}}\left(4, M_{t}, 6\right), M_{t}=1,2,3,4
\end{aligned}
$$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}
$$
\begin{equation*}
i=s \bmod N_{w}, \quad s=0 \ldots N_{s u b}-1 \tag{316}
\end{equation*}
$$
where $N_{\text {sub }}$ denotes the number of physical subbands across the entire system bandwidth.
The values of $N_{w}$ used for non-adaptive precoding in UL DLRU and NLRU are given in Table 6-295. The values of $N_{w}$ used for non-adaptive precoding in UL SLRU are given in Table 6-296.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-295-Values of $\boldsymbol{N}_{\boldsymbol{w}}$ for non-adaptive precoding in UL DLRU and NLRU}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline Rank & $\mathbf{1}$ & $\mathbf{2}$ & $\mathbf{3}$ & $\mathbf{4}$ \\
\hline$N_{t}=2$ & 2 & 1 & N/A & N/A \\
\hline$N_{t}=4$ & 4 & 4 & 4 & 4 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-296-Values of $\boldsymbol{N}_{\boldsymbol{w}}$ for non-adaptive precoding in UL SLRU}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline Rank & $\mathbf{1}$ & $\mathbf{2}$ & $\mathbf{3}$ & $\mathbf{4}$ \\
\hline$N_{t}=2$ & 8 & 4 & $\mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{A}$ & $\mathrm{N} / \mathrm{A}$ \\
\hline$N_{t}=4$ & 64 & 64 & 64 & 64 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\subsection*{6.3.9.1.2.2 Adaptive precoding}

There is no precoding if there is only one transmit antenna at the AMS.
With adaptive precoding, the precoder $\mathbf{W}$ is derived at the ABS or at the AMS, as instructed by the ABS.
With 2-Tx or 4-Tx at the AMS in FDD and TDD systems, unitary codebook based adaptive precoding is supported. In this mode, an AMS transmits a sounding signal on the uplink to assist the precoder selection at the ABS. The ABS then signals the uplink precoding matrix index to be used by the AMS in the UL Basic Assignment A-MAP IE and UL Subband Assignment A-MAP IE.

With 2-Tx or 4-Tx at the AMS in TDD systems, adaptive precoding based on the measurements of downlink reference signals is supported. The AMS chooses the precoder based on the downlink measurements. The form and derivation of the precoding matrix does not need to be known at the ABS.

\subsection*{6.3.9.1.3 Uplink MIMO transmission modes}

There are five MIMO transmission modes for UL MIMO transmission as listed in Table 6-297.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-297-Uplink MIMO modes}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Mode index & Description & MIMO encoding format (MEF) & MIMO precoding \\
\hline Mode 0 & OL SU-MIMO & SFBC & Non-adaptive \\
\hline Mode 1 & OL SU-MIMO (SM) & VE & Non-adaptive \\
\hline Mode 2 & CL SU-MIMO (SM) & VE & Adaptive \\
\hline Mode 3 & OL Collaborative spatial multiplexing (MU-MIMO) & VE & Non-adaptive \\
\hline Mode 4 & CL Collaborative spatial multiplexing (MU-MIMO) & VE & Adaptive \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The allowed values of the parameters for each UL MIMO mode are shown in Table 6-298.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-298-UL MIMO parameters}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline & Number of transmit antennas & STC rate per MIMO layer & Number of MIMO streams & Number of subcarriers & Number of MIMO layers \\
\hline & $N_{t}$ & $R$ & $M_{t}$ & $N_{F}$ & $L$ \\
\hline \multirow{2}{*}{MIMO mode 0} & 2 & 1 & 2 & 2 & 1 \\
\hline & 4 & 1 & 2 & 2 & 1 \\
\hline MIMO mode 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-298-UL MIMO parameters (continued)}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline & Number of transmit antennas & STC rate per MIMO layer & Number of MIMO streams & Number of subcarriers & Number of MIMO layers \\
\hline & $N_{t}$ & $R$ & $M_{t}$ & $N_{F}$ & $L$ \\
\hline \multirow{6}{*}{MIMO mode 1 and MIMO mode 2} & 2 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\
\hline & 2 & 2 & 2 & 1 & 1 \\
\hline & 4 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\
\hline & 4 & 2 & 2 & 1 & 1 \\
\hline & 4 & 3 & 3 & 1 & 1 \\
\hline & 4 & 4 & 4 & 1 & 1 \\
\hline \multirow[t]{7}{*}{MIMO mode 3 and MIMO mode 4} & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\
\hline & 2 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\
\hline & 2 & 2 & 2 & 1 & 1 \\
\hline & 4 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\
\hline & 4 & 2 & 2 & 1 & 1 \\
\hline & 4 & 3 & 3 & 1 & 1 \\
\hline & 4 & 4 & 4 & 1 & 1 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}
$M_{t}$ refers to the number of MIMO streams transmitted from one AMS.
In mode 3 and 4, $N_{t}$ refers to the number of transmit antennas at one AMS involved in CSM.

\subsection*{6.3.9.2 Transmission schemes for data channels}

\subsection*{6.3.9.2.1 Encoding and precoding of SU-MIMO modes}

\subsection*{6.3.9.2.1.1 Encoding of SU-MIMO modes}
- MIMO mode $\mathbf{0}$ : SFBC encoding of 6.3.9.1.1.1 shall be used with MIMO mode 0 .
- MIMO mode 1: Vertical encoding of 6.3.9.1.1.2 shall be used with MIMO mode 1. The number of MIMO streams is $M_{t} \leq \min \left(N_{t}, N_{r}\right)$, where $N_{r}$ is the number of receive antennas and $M_{t}$ is no more than 4.
- MIMO mode 2: Vertical encoding of 6.3.9.1.1.2 shall be used with MIMO mode 2. The number of MIMO streams is $M_{t} \leq \min \left(N_{t}, N_{r}\right)$, where $M_{t}$ is no more than 4 .

\subsection*{6.3.9.2.1.2 Precoding of SU-MIMO modes}
- MIMO mode 0: Non-adaptive precoding with $M_{t}=2$ MIMO streams shall be used with MIMO mode 0.
- MIMO mode 1: Non-adaptive precoding with $M_{t}$ MIMO streams shall be used with MIMO mode 1.
- MIMO mode 2: Adaptive precoding shall be used with MIMO mode 2.

\subsection*{6.3.9.2.2 Encoding and precoding of collaborative spatial multiplexing (MU-MIMO)}

AMSs can perform collaborative spatial multiplexing onto the same RU. In this case, the ABS assigns different pilot patterns for each AMS.

\subsection*{6.3.9.2.2.1 Encoding of MU-MIMO modes}
- MIMO mode 3: Vertical encoding shall be used with MIMO mode 3.
- MIMO mode 4: Vertical encoding shall be used with MIMO mode 4.

\subsection*{6.3.9.2.2.2 Precoding of MU-MIMO modes}
- MIMO mode 3: Non-adaptive precoding shall be used with MIMO mode 3.
- MIMO mode 4: Adaptive precoding shall be used with MIMO mode 4.

\subsection*{6.3.9.2.3 Mapping of data subcarriers}

Consecutive symbols for each antenna at the output of the MIMO precoder are mapped in a frequency domain first order across LRUs of the allocation, starting from the data subcarrier with the smallest OFDM symbol index and smallest subcarrier index, and continuing to subcarrier index with increasing subcarrier index. When the edge of the allocation is reached, the mapping is continued on the next OFDM symbol.

\subsection*{6.3.9.2.4 Usage of MIMO modes}

Table 6-299 shows the permutations supported for each MIMO mode.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-299-Supported permutation for each UL MIMO mode}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline & DLRU & NLRU & SLRU \\
\hline MIMO mode 0 & Yes & Yes & Yes \\
\hline MIMO mode 1 & Yes, with $M_{t} \leq 2$ & Yes & Yes \\
\hline MIMO mode 2 & Yes, with $M_{t} \leq 2$ & Yes & Yes \\
\hline MIMO mode 3 & Yes, with $M_{t}=1$ & Yes & Yes \\
\hline MIMO mode 4 & Yes, with $M_{t}=1$ & Yes & Yes \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\subsection*{6.3.9.2.5 Downlink signaling support of UL-MIMO modes}

\subsection*{6.3.9.2.5.1 Broadcast information}

The ABS shall send parameters necessary for UL MIMO operation in a unicast message. The parameters may be transmitted depending on the type of operation. The unicast information is carried in the UL basic Assignment A-MAP IE, UL Subband Assignment A-MAP IE, and UL Persistent A-MAP IE.

Table 6-300 specifies the DL control parameters required for UL MIMO operation.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-300-UL MIMO control parameters}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Parameter & Description & Value & Notes \\
\hline MEF & MIMO Encoding Format & SFBC Vertical encoding & MIMO encoding format \\
\hline CSM & Collaborative Spatial Multiplexing & Disabled or enabled & SU MIMO if CSM is disabled. MU MIMO if CSM is enabled. \\
\hline $M_{t}$ & Number of MIMO streams & 1 to 4 & Number of MIMO streams in the AMS transmission. \\
\hline TNS & Total number of MIMO streams in the LRU & 1 to 8 & Enabled when CSM is enabled. Indication of the total number of MIMO streams in the LRU. \\
\hline SI & First pilot index & 1 to 8 & Enabled when CSM is enabled. \\
\hline PF & Precoding flag & No-adaptive precoding or adaptive codebook precoding & Cannot be applied to the AMS with 1 transmit antenna. \\
\hline PMI Indicator & PMI indicator & 0b0: the AMS shall use the precoder of rank $M_{t}$ of its choice 0b1: the indicated PMI of rank $M_{t}$ shall be used by the AMS for precoding & This field is relevant only when PF indicates adaptive codebook precoding. PMI indication = 0b0 may be used in TDD When PMI indication = 0b1, the ABS selects the precoder to use at the AMS. \\
\hline PMI & Precoding matrix index in the UL base codebook & 0 to 9 when $N_{t}=2$ 0 to 63 when $N_{t}=4$ & Enabled when PF indicates adaptive codebook precoding, and PMI indication $=0 \mathrm{~b} 1$. \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\subsection*{6.3.9.3 Codebook for closed-loop transmit precoding}

The notation $C_{\text {base }, U L}\left(N_{t}, M_{t}, N B\right)$ denotes the rank- $M_{t}$ uplink base codebook, which consists of $2^{N B}$ complex matrices of dimension $N_{t}$ by $M_{t}$, and $M_{t}$ denotes the number of MIMO streams.

The notation $C_{\text {base }, U L}\left(N_{t}, M_{t}, N B, i\right)$ denotes the $i$-th codebook entry of $C_{\text {base }, U L}\left(N_{t}, M_{t}, N B\right)$.

\subsection*{6.3.9.3.1 Base codebook for two transmit antenna}

\subsection*{6.3.9.3.1.1 SU-MIMO base codebook}

The base codebooks of SU-MIMO with two transmit antennas consist of rank-1 codebook $C_{\text {base, } U L}(2,1,4)$ and rank-2 codebook $C_{\text {base, } U L}(2,2,3)$. Table 6-301 is included to illustrate the rank-1 base codebooks.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-301-C base, UL $(\mathbf{2 , 1 , 4})$}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline \multirow{2}{*}{Binary index} & \multirow{2}{*}{$m$} & \multicolumn{2}{|c|}{$C_{\text {base }, U L}(2,1,4, m)=\left[c_{1} ; c_{2}\right]$} \\
\hline & & $\boldsymbol{c}_{\mathbf{1}}$ & $c_{2}$ \\
\hline 0000 & 0 & 0.7071 & -0.7071 \\
\hline 0001 & 1 & 0.7071 & $-0.5000-0.5000 \mathrm{i}$ \\
\hline 0010 & 2 & 0.7071 & -0.7071i \\
\hline 0011 & 3 & 0.7071 & 0.5000-0.5000i \\
\hline 0100 & 4 & 0.7071 & 0.7071 \\
\hline 0101 & 5 & 0.7071 & $0.5000+0.5000 \mathrm{i}$ \\
\hline 0110 & 6 & 0.7071 & 0.7071i \\
\hline 0111 & 7 & 0.7071 & $-0.5000+0.5000 i$ \\
\hline 1000 & 8 & 1 & 0 \\
\hline 1001 & 9 & 0 & 1 \\
\hline 1010-1111 & 10-15 & - & - \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The rank-2 base codebook $C_{\text {base }, U L}(2,2,3)$ for uplink 2-Tx is the same as the downlink 2-Tx rank-2 base codebook.

\subsection*{6.3.9.3.1.2 MU-MIMO base codebook}

The base codebook for UL collaborative spatial multiplexing MIMO is the same as the base codebook for SU-MIMO, defined in 6.3.9.3.1.1.

\subsection*{6.3.9.3.2 Base codebook for four transmit antennas}

\subsection*{6.3.9.3.2.1 SU-MIMO base codebook}

The uplink base codebook of SU-MIMO with four transmit antennas consist of rank-1 codebook $C_{\text {base }, U L}(4,1,6)$, rank-2 codebook $C_{\text {base }, U L}(4,2,6)$, rank-3 codebook $C_{\text {base }, U L}(4,3,6)$, and rank-4 codebook $C_{\text {base, } U L}(4,4,6)$. Rank-1 codebook entry $C_{\text {base, } U L}(4,1,6, m)$ consists of the first column of $C_{\text {base, } U L}(4,4,6, m)$. Rank-2 codebook entry $C_{\text {base }, U L}(4,2,6, m)$ consists of the first two columns of $C_{\text {base }, U L}(4,4,6, m)$. Rank-3 codebook entry $C_{\text {base }, U L}(4,3,6, m)$ consists of the first three columns of $C_{\text {base }, U L}(4,4,6, m)$. Table 6-302 specifies the rank-4 base codebook.

\subsection*{6.3.9.3.2.2 MU-MIMO base codebook}

The base codebook for UL collaborative spatial multiplexing MIMO is a 4-bit subset of the base codebook for UL SU-MIMO, defined in 6.3.9.3.2.1. The codebook is defined in Table 6-303 for rank-1, -2, and -3.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-302-C base, UL $(\mathbf{4 , 4 , 6 )}$}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline \multirow[t]{6}{*}{Binary Index} & \multirow{6}{*}{$m$} & \multicolumn{4}{|c|}{\multirow[b]{2}{*}{$C_{\text {base }, U L}(4,4,6, m)=\left[\begin{array}{llll}c_{11} & c_{12} & c_{13} & c_{14} \\ c_{21} & c_{22} & c_{23} & c_{24} \\ c_{31} & c_{32} & c_{33} & c_{34} \\ c_{41} & c_{42} & c_{43} & c_{44}\end{array}\right]$}} \\
\hline & & & & & \\
\hline & & $\mathrm{c}_{11}$ & $\mathrm{c}_{12}$ & $\mathrm{c}_{13}$ & $\mathrm{c}_{14}$ \\
\hline & & $\mathrm{c}_{21}$ & $\mathrm{c}_{22}$ & $\mathrm{c}_{23}$ & $\mathrm{c}_{24}$ \\
\hline & & $\mathrm{c}_{31}$ & $\mathrm{c}_{32}$ & $\mathrm{c}_{33}$ & $\mathrm{c}_{34}$ \\
\hline & & $\mathrm{c}_{41}$ & $\mathrm{c}_{42}$ & $\mathrm{c}_{43}$ & $\mathrm{c}_{44}$ \\
\hline \multirow[t]{4}{*}{000000} & \multirow[t]{4}{*}{0} & 0.5000 & 0.5000 & 0.5000 & 0.5000 \\
\hline & & 0.5000 i & -0.5000 & -0.5000i & 0.5000 \\
\hline & & -0.5000 & 0.5000 & -0.5000 & 0.5000 \\
\hline & & -0.5000i & -0.5000 & 0.5000 i & 0.5000 \\
\hline \multirow[t]{4}{*}{000001} & \multirow[t]{4}{*}{1} & 0.5000 & 0.5000 & 0.5000 & 0.5000 \\
\hline & & 0.2357-0.4410i & $0.4410+0.2357 \mathrm{i}$ & $-0.2357+0.4410 \mathrm{i}$ & -0.4410-0.2357i \\
\hline & & -0.4619-0.1913i & $0.4619+0.1913 \mathrm{i}$ & -0.4619-0.1913i & $0.4619+0.1913 \mathrm{i}$ \\
\hline & & -0.4619-0.1913i & $-0.1913+0.4619 i$ & $0.4619+0.1913 \mathrm{i}$ & 0.1913-0.4619i \\

\subsection*{6.3.9.4 Codebook subsets for open-loop transmit precoding}

\subsection*{6.3.9.4.1 OL SU-MIMO subset}

The UL OL SU-MIMO codebook subset shall be used for non-adaptive precoding with MIMO mode 0 and MIMO mode 1 in DLRU and NLRU.

The notation $C_{U L, O L, S U}\left(N_{t}, M_{t}, N_{w}\right)$ denotes the UL OL SU-MIMO codebook subset, which consists of $N_{w}$ complex matrices of dimension $N_{t}$ by $M_{t}$, and $M_{t}$ denotes the number of MIMO streams. The notation $C_{U L, O L, S U}\left(N_{t}, M_{t}, N_{w}, i\right)$ denotes the $i$-th codebook entry of $C_{U L, O L, S U}\left(N_{t}, M_{t}, N_{w}\right)$.
$C_{U L, O L, S U}\left(N_{t}, M_{t}, N_{w}\right)$ shall be used for precoding with $N_{t}$ transmit antennas and $M_{t}$ MIMO streams with MIMO mode 0 and MIMO mode 1.

\subsection*{6.3.9.4.1.1 OL SU-MIMO subset for two transmit antennas}

The UL OL SU-MIMO codebook subset for 2-Tx is the same as the DL OL SU-MIMO codebook subset for 2-Tx. $C_{U L, O L, S U}\left(2, M_{t}, N_{w}\right)=C_{D L, O L, S U}\left(2, M_{t}, N_{w}\right)$, and it shall be used for precoding with 2 transmit antennas and $M_{t}$ MIMO streams with MIMO mode 0 and MIMO mode 1.

\subsection*{6.3.9.4.1.2 OL SU-MIMO subset for four transmit antennas}

The codewords $C_{U L, O L, S U}\left(4, M_{t}, 4, n\right)$ of the OL SU-MIMO codebook subset for four transmit antennas, $C_{U L, O L, S U}\left(4, M_{t}, 4\right)$ are given in Table 6-304 for each rank $M_{t}$. The corresponding codewords $C_{\text {base, } U L}\left(4, M_{t}, 6, m\right)$ of the uplink base codebook for four transmit antennas $C_{\text {base, } U L}\left(4, M_{t}, 6\right)$ are given in Table 6-304.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-304-C ${ }_{U L, O L, S U}(4,1,4), C_{U L, O L, S U}(4,2,4), C_{U L, O L, S U}(4,3,4)$, and $C_{U L, O L, S U}(4,4,4)$}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline \multicolumn{2}{|c|}{$\left.\boldsymbol{C}_{\boldsymbol{U} l, \text { Ol, SU }} \boldsymbol{(} \mathbf{4}, \mathbf{1}, \mathbf{4}, n\right)$} & \multicolumn{2}{|c|}{$\boldsymbol{C}_{\boldsymbol{U} L, O L, S U}(\mathbf{4}, \mathbf{2}, \mathbf{4}, n)$} & \multicolumn{2}{|c|}{$\boldsymbol{C}_{\boldsymbol{U} \boldsymbol{L} \boldsymbol{,} \boldsymbol{O L} \boldsymbol{,} \boldsymbol{S U} \boldsymbol{U}} \boldsymbol{(} \mathbf{4} \boldsymbol{,} \mathbf{3} \boldsymbol{,} \mathbf{4} \boldsymbol{,} \boldsymbol{n} \boldsymbol{)}$} & \multicolumn{2}{|c|}{$\boldsymbol{C}_{\boldsymbol{U} \boldsymbol{L}, \boldsymbol{O} \boldsymbol{L} \boldsymbol{,} \boldsymbol{S} \boldsymbol{U}} \boldsymbol{(} \mathbf{4}, \mathbf{4}, \mathbf{4}, \boldsymbol{n} \boldsymbol{)}$} \\
\hline $n$ & $C_{\text {base }, U L}(4,1,6, m)$ & $n$ & $C_{\text {base }, U L}(4,2,6, m)$ & $n$ & $C_{\text {base }, U L}(4,3,6, m)$ & $n$ & $C_{\text {base }, U L}(4,4,6, m)$ \\
\hline 0 & $C_{\text {base }, U L}(4,1,6,9)$ & 0 & $C_{\text {base }, U L}(4,2,6,9)$ & 0 & $C_{\text {base, } U L}(4,3,6,9)$ & 0 & $C_{\text {base }, U L}(4,4,6,9)$ \\
\hline 1 & $C_{\text {base, } U L}(4,1,6,15)$ & 1 & $C_{\text {base, } U L}(4,2,6,15)$ & 1 & $C_{\text {base, } U L}(4,3,6,15)$ & 1 & $C_{\text {base, } U L}(4,4,6,15)$ \\
\hline 2 & $C_{\text {base, } U L}(4,1,6,49)$ & 2 & $C_{\text {base, } U L}(4,2,6,49)$ & 2 & $C_{\text {base, } U L}(4,3,6,49)$ & 2 & $C_{\text {base, } U L}(4,4,6,49)$ \\
\hline 3 & $C_{\text {base, } U L}(4,1,6,55)$ & 3 & $C_{\text {base, } U L}(4,2,6,55)$ & 3 & $C_{\text {base, } U L}(4,3,6,55)$ & 3 & $C_{\text {base, } U L}(4,4,6,55)$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\subsection*{6.3.9.4.2 OL MU-MIMO subset}

The UL OL MU-MIMO codebook subset is same as the UL OL SU-MIMO codebook subset.

\subsection*{6.3.10 Channel coding and HARQ}

\subsection*{6.3.10.1 Channel coding for the data channel}

Channel coding procedures for downlink and uplink data channels are shown in Figure 6-198.

\begin{figure}
\includegraphics[alt={},max width=\textwidth]{https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/b75642f5-8f88-4574-9a75-f32eadecb271-0878.jpg?height=124&width=1470&top_left_y=1907&top_left_x=339}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Figure 6-198-Channel coding procedure for data channel}
\end{figure}

\subsection*{6.3.10.1.1 Burst CRC encoding}

Cyclic Redundancy Code (CRC) bits are used to detect errors in the received packets. A 16-bit burst CRC, per the CRC16-CCITT specification in Rec. ITU-T X.25, shall be appended to the data burst using the cyclic generator polynomial in Equation (317) if the number of FEC block partitions $K_{F B}$ is larger than 1:
$$
\begin{equation*}
g_{D B-C R C}(D)=D^{16}+D^{12}+D^{5}+1 \tag{317}
\end{equation*}
$$

As an example, if the input data of 4 bytes in hexadecimal is 0 x 1234 ABCD , the burst CRC encoding above yields 16-bit burst CRC of 0x5DF0. Note that this is just for the purpose of giving an example for the burst CRC encoding itself and the burst CRC shall be appended only when the data burst is partitioned into multiple FEC blocks (or $K_{F B}>1$ ).

The data burst, including the CRC if appended when $K_{F B}>1$, is further processed by the burst partition as described in 6.3.10.1.2.

\subsection*{6.3.10.1.2 Burst partition}

Only the burst sizes $N_{D B}$ listed in Table 6-305 are supported in the PHY layer. These sizes include the addition of CRC (per burst and/or per FEC block), when applicable. Other sizes require padding (refer to 6.2.4.7) to the next burst size excluding FEC CRC and burst CRC. When the burst size including 16 CRC bits for a data burst and/or FEC blocks exceeds the maximum FEC block size, $N_{F B \_M A X}$, the burst is partitioned into $K_{F B}$ FEC blocks.

\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-305-Burst size}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline idx & $\boldsymbol{N}_{\boldsymbol{D} \boldsymbol{B}}$ (byte) & $\boldsymbol{K}_{\boldsymbol{F B}}$ & $i d x$ & $\boldsymbol{N}_{\boldsymbol{D B}}$ (byte) & $\boldsymbol{K}_{\boldsymbol{F B}}$ & $i d x$ & $\boldsymbol{N}_{\boldsymbol{D} \boldsymbol{B}}$ (byte) & $\boldsymbol{K}_{\boldsymbol{F B}}$ \\
\hline 1 & 6 & 1 & 23 & 90 & 1 & 45 & 1200 & 2 \\
\hline 2 & 8 & 1 & 24 & 100 & 1 & 46 & 1416 & 3 \\
\hline 3 & 9 & 1 & 25 & 114 & 1 & 47 & 1584 & 3 \\

\hline 12 & 25 & 1 & 34 & 328 & 1 & 56 & 4800 & 8 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\caption{Table 6-305-Burst size (continued)}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline idx & $\boldsymbol{N}_{\boldsymbol{D} \boldsymbol{B}}$ (byte) & $\boldsymbol{K}_{\boldsymbol{F B}}$ & $i d x$ & $\boldsymbol{N}_{\boldsymbol{D B}}$ (byte) & $\boldsymbol{K}_{\boldsymbol{F B}}$ & $i d x$ & $\boldsymbol{N}_{\boldsymbol{D} \boldsymbol{B}}$ (byte) & $\boldsymbol{K}_{\boldsymbol{F B}}$ \\
\hline 13 & 27 & 1 & 35 & 368 & 1 & 57 & 5400 & 9 \\

\hline 21 & 71 & 1 & 43 & 944 & 2 & 65 & 12000 & 20 \\

The burst size index is calculated as
$$
i d x=I_{\text {MinimalSize }}+I_{\text {SizeOffset }}
$$
where $\quad I_{\text {SizeOffset }} \in\{0,1, \ldots, 31\} \quad$ is a 5 bits index in A-MAP IE, and $I_{\text {MinimalSize }}$ is calculated based on the allocation size as shown in Table 6-306. The allocation size is defined as the number of LRUs multiplied by the STC rate allocated for the burst. In the case of long TTI, the number of LRUs is calculated as the number of AAI subframes in TTI multiplied by the number of LRUs per AAI subframe. For allocation size 185~192, $I_{\text {SizeOffset }}=31$ is invalid.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-306-Minimal size index as a function of the allocation size}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Allocation size & $I_{\text {MinimalSize }}$ & Allocation size & $I_{\text {MinimalSize }}$ & Allocation size & $I_{\text {MinimalSize }}$ \\
\hline $1 \sim 3$ & 1 & 16 ~ 18 & 15 & $58 \sim 64$ & 26 \\
\hline 4 & 2 & 19 ~ 20 & 16 & 65 ~ 72 & 27 \\
\hline 5 & 4 & 21~22 & 17 & 73 ~ 82 & 28 \\
\hline 6 & 6 & 23~25 & 18 & 83 ~ 90 & 29 \\
\hline 7 & 8 & 26 ~ 28 & 19 & $91 \sim 102$ & 30 \\
\hline 8 & 9 & 29 ~ 32 & 20 & 103 ~ 116 & 31 \\
\hline 9 & 10 & 33~35 & 21 & 117~131 & 32 \\
\hline 10~11 & 11 & $36 \sim 40$ & 22 & 132 ~ 145 & 33 \\
\hline 12 & 12 & $41 \sim 45$ & 23 & 146 ~ 164 & 34 \\
\hline 13 & 13 & $46 \sim 50$ & 24 & 165 ~ 184 & 35 \\
\hline 14 ~ 15 & 14 & 51~57 & 25 & 185 ~ 192 & 36 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The modulation order $N_{\text {mod }}$ (2 for QPSK, 4 for 16-QAM and 6 for 64-QAM) depends on the parameter $I_{\text {SizeOffset }}$ and AAI subframe type according to Table 6-307 or Table 6-308. Allocation size of 1 or 2 are special cases (separate columns in the table). For allocation size of at least 3 , the modulation order depends only on $I_{\text {SizeOffset }}$. The allocation size and the value of $I_{\text {SizeOffset }}$ are set by the ABS scheduler, which takes into account the resulting modulation order and effective code rate, and according to the link adaptation.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-307-Rules for modulation order (for default TTI burst transmitted other than Type 3 subframe or long-TTI burst transmitted over subframes not including Type 3 subframe)}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline $\boldsymbol{I}_{\text {SizeOffset }}$ & $\boldsymbol{N}_{\text {mod }}$ (allocation size > 2) & $\boldsymbol{N}_{\text {mod }}$ (allocation size $=2$ ) & $\boldsymbol{N}_{\text {mod }}$ (allocation size = 1) \\
\hline $0 \sim 9$ & 2 & 2 & 2 \\
\hline 10 ~ 15 & 2 & 2 & 4 \\
\hline 16~18 & 2 & 4 & 6 \\
\hline 19~21 & 4 & 4 & 6 \\
\hline 22~23 & 4 & 6 & 6 \\
\hline 24 ~ 31 & 6 & 6 & 6 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 6-308-Rules for modulation order (for default TTI burst transmitted in Type 3 subframe or long-TTI burst transmitted over subframes including Type 3 subframe)}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline $\boldsymbol{I}_{\text {SizeOffset }}$ & $\boldsymbol{N}_{\text {mod }}$ (allocation size > 2) & $\boldsymbol{N}_{\text {mod }}$ (allocation size = 2) & $\boldsymbol{N}_{\text {mod }}$ (allocation size = 1) \\
\hline $0 \sim 7$ & 2 & 2 & 2 \\
\hline $8 \sim 13$ & 2 & 2 & 4 \\
\hline 14~16 & 2 & 4 & 6 \\
\hline 17~19 & 4 & 4 & 6 \\
\hline $20 \sim 21$ & 4 & 6 & 6 \\
\hline 22~31 & 6 & 6 & 6 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

In broadcast assignment A-MAP IE, the burst size index is directly signaled as 'Burst Size' instead of being indirectly derived using $I_{\text {SizeOffset }}$ and $I_{\text {Minimalsize }}$. When the uplink BW for BR header is granted through CDMA allocation A-MAP IE, neither $I_{\text {SizeOffset }}$ nor burst size index is signaled, but the value of burst size index is predetermined as 2 (i.e., 8 bytes). In both cases, the burst size index and the modulation order are not dependent on the allocation size, where the modulation order $N_{\text {mod }}$ is fixed to 2 (i.e., QPSK).

In GRA A-MAP IE, the Resource Allocation Bitmap is used to inform both the burst size and the resource size as specified in 6.3.5.5.2.4.10. The burst size is the one among the four burst sizes predefined in the AAI-

GRP-CFG message. The resource size is the one among the eight resource sizes predefined in the AAI-GRP-CFG message. The modulation order $N_{\text {mod }}$ is dependent on the burst size and the resource size signaled in GRA A-MAP IE. The burst size index is found in Table 6-304 for the signaled burst size and $I_{\text {Minimalsize }}$ is found in Table 6-305 after calculating the allocation size for the signaled resource size. $I_{\text {SizeOffset }}$ is calculated as subtracting $I_{\text {Minimalsize }}$ from the burst size index, and $N_{\text {mod }}$ is found in Table 6-306.

If a burst is partitioned into more than one FEC block, each partitioned FEC block has the same size. The size of the FEC encoder input is denoted by $N_{F B}$. The set of supported FEC encoder input sizes including FEC block CRC, when applicable, is the subset of the burst size table, i.e., $N_{D B}$ of $i d x$ from 1 to 39 in Table 6-305.

The burst size $N_{D B}$ including burst CRC and FEC block CRC is defined by Equation (318).
$$
\begin{equation*}
N_{D B}=K_{F B} \times N_{F B} \tag{318}
\end{equation*}
$$

The payload size excluding burst CRC and FEC block CRC is defined by Equation (319).
$$
\begin{equation*}
N_{P L}=N_{D B}-I_{M F B} \times N_{D B-C R C}-K_{F B} \times N_{F B-C R C} \tag{319}
\end{equation*}
$$
where

\begin{tabular}{ll}
$I_{M F B}$ & equals 0 when $K_{F B}=1,1$ when $K_{F B}>1$. \\
$N_{F B-C R C}$ & equals 16, which is the size of the FEC block CRC. \\
$N_{D B-C R C}$ & equals 16, which is the size of the burst CRC.
\end{tabular}

The burst partition block generates $K_{F B}$ FEC blocks, with each FEC block processed by the randomization block as described in 6.3.10.1.3.

\subsection*{6.3.10.1.3 Randomization}

The randomization bits are generated using a PRBS generator as shown in Figure 6-199. The generator polynomial of the PRBS generator is $1+X^{14}+X^{15}$. The initial vector of the PRBS generator shall be designated [011011100010101] from LSB to MSB.

The data byte to be transmitted shall enter sequentially into the randomization, MSB first. The data bits are XOR-ed with the output of the PRBS generator, with the MSB of the data burst XOR-ed with the first bit of the PRBS generator output.

The randomization is initialized with the initial vector for each FEC block.

\begin{figure}
\includegraphics[alt={},max width=\textwidth]{https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/b75642f5-8f88-4574-9a75-f32eadecb271-0882.jpg?height=411&width=1283&top_left_y=1976&top_left_x=408}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Figure 6-199-Data randomization with a PRBS generator}
\end{figure}

The output of the data randomization is further processed by FEC block CRC addition block as described in 6.3.10.1.4.

\subsection*{6.3.10.1.4 FEC block CRC encoding}

The burst partition procedure generates $K_{F B}$ FEC blocks for each burst. The FEC block CRC generator appends a 16-bit FEC block CRC for each FEC block. For the 16-bit FEC block CRC, CRC16-ANSI is used with the CRC register preset to all-zeros but without post-inversion of the CRC encoding result. The cyclic generator for FEC block CRC encoding is shown in Equation (320).
$$
\begin{equation*}
g_{F B-C R C}(D)=D^{16}+D^{15}+D^{2}+1 \tag{320}
\end{equation*}
$$

Denote the bits of FEC encoder input by $d_{l}, d_{2}, \ldots, d_{N_{F B}}$ with $d_{1}$ being the MSB and $N_{F B}$ being the size of the FEC encoder input, including the 16 -bit FEC block CRC. Denote the parity bits produced by the FEC block CRC generator by $p_{1}, p_{2}, \ldots, p_{16}$. The FEC block CRC encoding is performed in a systematic form, which means that in $\mathrm{GF}(2)$, the polynomial in Equation (321).
$$
\begin{equation*}
d_{1} D^{N_{F B}-1}+d_{2} D^{N_{F B}-2}+\ldots+d_{N_{F B}-16} D^{16}+p_{1} D^{15}+p_{2} D^{14}+\ldots+p_{15} D^{1}+p_{16} \tag{321}
\end{equation*}
$$
yields a remainder equal to 0 when divided by $g_{F B-C R C}(D)$.

As an example, if the input data of 4 bytes in hexadecimal is 0 x1234ABCD, the FEC block CRC encoding above yields 16-bit CRC of 0x9332.

\subsection*{6.3.10.1.5 FEC encoding}

Each FEC block shall be encoded using the convolutional turbo codes specified in 6.3.10.1.5.1.

\subsection*{6.3.10.1.5.1 Convolutional turbo codes}

\section*{CTC encoder}

The CTC encoder, including its constituent encoder, is depicted in Figure 6-200.

It uses a double binary CRSC (Circular Recursive Systematic Convolutional) code.

The bits of the data to be encoded are alternatively fed to A and B , starting with the MSB of the first byte being fed to A , followed by the next bit being fed to B . The encoder is fed by blocks of $N_{F B}$ bits or $N$ couples ( $N_{F B}=2 N$ bits).

The polynomials defining the connections are described in octal and symbol notations as follows:
For the feedback branch: 13 , equivalently $1+D+D^{3}$ (in octal notation)
For the Y parity bit: 15 , equivalently $1+D^{2}+D^{3}$
For the W parity bit: 11 , equivalently $1+D^{3}$

\begin{figure}
\includegraphics[alt={},max width=\textwidth]{https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/b75642f5-8f88-4574-9a75-f32eadecb271-0884.jpg?height=869&width=1294&top_left_y=330&top_left_x=440}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Figure 6-200-CTC encoder}
\end{figure}

First, the encoder (after initialization by the circulation state $S_{c}$; see 8.4.9.2.3 in IEEE Std 802.16) is fed the sequence in the natural order (switch 1 in Figure 6-200) with incremental address $i=0,1, \ldots, N-1$. This first encoding is called $C_{1}$ encoding.

Next, the encoder (after initialization by the circulation state $S_{c}$; see 8.4.9.2.3.3 in IEEE Std 802.16) is fed by the interleaved sequence (switch 2 in Figure 6-200) with incremental address $i=0,1, \ldots, N-1$. This second encoding is called $C_{2}$ encoding.

The order in which the encoded bits shall be fed into the bit separation block (see 8.4.9.2.3.4 in IEEE Std 802.16) is as follows:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{A}, \mathrm{~B}, \mathrm{Y}_{1}, \mathrm{Y}_{2}, \mathrm{~W}_{1}, \mathrm{~W}_{2}= \\
& \quad A_{0}, A_{1}, A_{2} \ldots, A_{N-1}, B_{0}, B_{1}, B_{2}, B_{N-1}, Y_{1,0}, Y_{1,1}, Y_{1,2}, \ldots Y_{1, N-1}, \\
& \quad Y_{2,0}, Y_{2,1}, Y_{2,2}, \ldots Y_{2, N-1}, W_{1,0}, W_{1,1}, W_{1,2}, \ldots W_{1, N-1}, W_{2,0}, W_{2,1}, W_{2,2}, \ldots W_{2, N-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

The CTC interleaver requires the parameters $P_{0}, P_{1}, P_{2}$, and $P_{3}$ shown in Table 6-309, where the value of $N_{F B}$ is shown in bit.

The detailed interleaver structures except table for interleaver parameters correspond to 8.4.9.2.3.2 in IEEE Std 802.16.
